supporting_evidence.text: RAP (retrieval of successful episodic trajectories) boosts ALFWorld/WebShop agent success rates by ~30-35% over ReAct baseline and outperforms both Reflexion (reflection-only) and ADaPT (decomposition/re-execution) benchmarks; RAP memory generalizes to new model architectures (e.g., Llama2-13b) and supports cross-modal retrieval plans.

MC-DML (in-trial + cross-trial episodic reflections as memory) sets new upper bounds on early planning performance in Jericho/IF games, with performance gains of 10-30 points over pure LLM-MCTS, Reflection-only, and LLM baselines; ablation shows both reflection and retrieval are needed for full effect.

Sweet&Sour (reflection on both successes and failures with managed memory buffer) yields higher success rates in ScienceWorld across all tested LLMs than failure-only reflection (Reflexion) or no-reflection baselines, with larger gains for smaller models.

Generative Agents (Simulacra) and ThinkThrice (retrieval + self-verification) show that externally stored experience, retrieval and structured reflection, and plan synthesis all contribute to emergent, coherent, and generalizable behavior in open yet partially observable environments.

In many settings (ALFWorld, WebShop), Reflexion/failure-only reflection improves over pure LLM baselines but is outperformed by retrieval-augmented or full episodic memory architectures (RAP, MC-DML, Sweet&Sour).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Reflection (Reflexion), retrieval (RAG, RAP), and plan-based retrieval exist separately in prior LLM agent literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The law asserts that memory banks supporting both reflection (verbal self-critique) and retrieval of relevant past trajectories/plans are not just helpful but strictly necessary for rapid adaptation and transfer in multi-step text games—supported across multiple environments and agent designs.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Though reflection and retrieval exist individually, the theoretical unification, task demonstrations, and role in transfer/generalization are new.
references: Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion [reflection/intra-task memory]
Papazian et al. (2024) RAP [retrieval of past episodic trajectories improves performance and generalization]
Hao et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [reflection on both positive and negative experience with buffer management]
Zhou et al. (2023) Generative Agents [memory + reflection + plan synthesis in multi-agent simulations]

theory_statements.statement_name: Positive and Negative Experience Reflection Principle
theory_statements.theory_statement: Providing agents with managed memory buffers containing both positive (successful subgoal) and negative (failure) reflections enhances learning, resilience, and out-of-distribution adaptability more substantially than failure-only reflective memory, especially in long-horizon or sparse-reward settings.
theory_statements.domain_scope: LLM-based agents in environments with extended episode lengths, subgoal structure, or delayed/non-stationary rewards (ScienceWorld, Jericho tasks, open-ended text-adventure/bot games).
special_cases: Reflection content must be accurate—if the agent's evaluation is unreliable or subgoal delineation is unavailable, the benefits of positive memory may not materialize.
If the environment is fully observable and trivial, positive/negative reflection may offer little incremental benefit.
For tasks demanding novelty/discovery (as opposed to repetition or analogy), prior reflection could sometimes hinder exploration (negative transfer).

supporting_evidence.text: Sweet&Sour's dual-buffer reflection (positive+negative) outperforms fail-only ablation and Reflexion on ScienceWorld—GPT-4o, Mistral Large 2, and Llama 8B all achieve higher average scores when both positive and negative experiences are reflected on and retrieved.
Reflexion tilts or gets stuck once initial success is achieved (cannot reinforce positives, neglects adaptation), Sweet&Sour avoids this pitfall.
MC-DML's cross-trial reflection, which incorporates both types of lesson, yields stronger ablation gains than reflection-only (failure-only) or prompt-only baselines in Jericho text games.
Simulacra baseline (inspired by Generative Agents' reasoning on importance/recency/success) outperforms simple context/retrieval on emergent behaviors.
Agents lacking memory of positive outcomes (fail-only) regress to less robust/tilted search, per-case and cross-paper demonstration.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Reflection on failure (Reflexion) exists, and separate positive experience utilization is common in traditional RL (reward shaping, memory buffers), but explicit positive/negative verbal reflection managed memory is novel for LLMs.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Demonstrating and formalizing the need for both positive and negative experience reflection (and their retrieval) as external, persistent memory in LLM agent architectures, including their impact on generalization and adaptation.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combination of both types of reflection in managed external memory for LLMs is novel and shown to be performance-critical.
references: Hao et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [explicit dual-buffer reflection, demonstration of necessity]
Shinn et al. (2023,2024) Reflexion [failure-only reflection as precedent, shown to be less effective]

new_predictions_likely: Adding structured reflection (positive and negative, task and subgoal) and memory retrieval to strong planning LLMs (e.g., ReAct, MC-DML) on new multi-step games or domains will improve sample efficiency and success rate on unseen tasks.
Failure-only or naive context-history memory will display sub-optimal or brittle behavior (tilting, local minima) in long-horizon or ambiguous reward environments.
Increasing the quality or coverage of stored reflections/trajectory logs will monotonically improve adaptability to out-of-distribution tasks, up to a saturation point.
new_predictions_unknown: Hierarchical or compositional reflection/retrieval (learning automatically which subgoals benefit from positive or negative memory) would further improve transfer performance.
Automating retrieval weighting/scoring by self-supervised learning (learning which memories are most relevant) could produce new reflect-retrieve hybrid architectures.
Highly stochastic or adversarial environments may benefit from added meta-reflection (reflection on reflection), potentially creating self-improving memory pipelines.
negative_experiments: Demonstration that LLM agents with reflection/retrieval memory perform identically to or worse than those without, when controlling for model/data/environment, would refute or limit the theory.
If the addition of positive/negative reflection memory induces instability or catastrophic forgetting (beyond known context-size tradeoffs), the principle is falsified.

If providing retrieval or reflection memory in highly creative/exploration facets (e.g., WebShop, ambiguous games) yields no improvement or active detriment, the boundary conditions of the theory are incorrect.

unaccounted_for.text: In tasks where all relevant knowledge is supplied in instruction/goals or immediate context and memory is unnecessary, benefits of reflection/retrieval would not manifest.
Optimal scaling and curation of memory banks (reflection/trajectory management) remain open—there may be domains where memory over-saturation hurts (prompt overflow, conflicting lessons).

conflicting_evidence.text: Some tasks with a near-unique solution path, or requiring creative generative exploration, are not improved by retrieval/reflective memory (e.g., Reflexion stagnates in WebShop, ambiguous games).

special_cases: Games/tasks with high stochasticity or major distribution shift between training reflection and test tasks may show negative transfer unless reflection retrieval is dynamically weighted or regularized.
If an agent is already successful in a task, additional failure reflection may have no effect or could even degrade performance ('tilt' effect—wrong lesson learning).

existing_theory.what_already_exists: Reflection-on-failure and RAG-style retrieval each exist for LLM agents in prior literature.
existing_theory.what_is_novel: Joint utilization of reflection (positive and negative) and trajectory/planning retrieval as external memory for effective sample-efficient, generalizable, and robust game-solving has not been previously formalized as a law.
existing_theory.classification_explanation: Components exist, but their unified memory-augmentation principle, including role of positive reflection and sample/transfer demonstration, is new.
references: Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion [failure-only reflection]
Papazian et al. (2024) RAP [retrieval-augmented planning]
Hao et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [positive+negative reflection memory]
Zhou et al. (2023) Generative Agents [retrieval and reflection in behavior simulation]

extraction-schema-1.extraction_query: Extract any mentions of language model agents augmented with memory for playing text games, including details of memory type, integration, and performance comparisons.

extracted_data.name_full: RoBERTa with Memory of Previous Correct Actions (MCA)
extracted_data.brief_description: A pre-trained encoder-only LM (roberta-large) fine-tuned as a multiple-choice action selector for ScienceWorld; augmented with a short-term episodic memory that records previous correct (rewarding) actions and appends them to the LM input using a sliding window.
extracted_data.agent_description: roberta-large is fine-tuned offline in a multiple-choice formulation: the current state (observation, inventory, task description) is concatenated with each candidate action and the LM scores them; at inference the LM scores all valid actions and top-p sampling is used to avoid loops.
extracted_data.memory_type: short-term episodic memory (Memory of Previous Correct Actions within the current episode)
extracted_data.memory_integration: context-window concatenation: MCA is serialized as a string and appended to the LM input; implemented with a sliding window to limit tokens (window size A = 5 in experiments)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: avg cumulative reward: 12.31 / 100 (RoBERTa with MCA, averaged across the 10 evaluated tasks; Table 2 'm' column average)
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: avg cumulative reward: 11.43 / 100 (RoBERTa baseline without MCA; Table 2 'b' column average)
extracted_data.key_findings: Appending a memory of previous correct actions (MCA) to RoBERTa yields a modest average improvement over the baseline (11.43 -> 12.31 avg cumulative reward, ~8% relative), but gains are task-dependent: MCA helped only a minority of tasks (3/10) and provided larger benefits when combined with affordance knowledge; MCA is limited by LM input length so a sliding window of recent correct actions (A=5) was used.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Memory must be derived from environment reward so it is unavailable at episode start; LM input token limits constrain the amount of episodic memory (necessitating a sliding window); MCA helped only some tasks and could be less effective than injecting affordances; RoBERTa required many more steps to finish in some cases (slower action selection) even when using MCA.

source_info.paper_title: Knowledge-enhanced Agents for Interactive Text Games

extracted_data.name_full: Swift (Flan-T5-based instruction-tuned LM) with action history
extracted_data.brief_description: An instruction-following Seq2Seq LM (Swift built on Flan-T5) that receives a structured state input including an action history field (last ten actions with rewards); the history can be included or omitted and the paper evaluates both variants.
extracted_data.agent_description: A Seq2Seq instruction-tuned LM (flan-T5-base) trained on state->action pairs; its input template includes desc, step number, score, action history, observation, inventory, visited rooms, and the prompt 'What action should you do next?'; the model generates the next action.
extracted_data.memory_type: short-term action history (last ten actions within episode, each annotated with reward)
extracted_data.memory_integration: structured Seq2Seq input field: the action history is included directly in the LM source sequence (the original Swift design uses the last ten actions with rewards); the paper also evaluated a variant where this history was omitted for comparison
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: avg cumulative reward: 24.65 / 100 (Swift with its original 10-action history included; Table 2 'm' column average for Swift)
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: avg cumulative reward: 27.86 / 100 (Swift baseline with the action history omitted for a fairer comparison; Table 2 'b' column average)
extracted_data.key_findings: Contrary to expectation, including the full 10-action history in Swift harmed average performance on these ScienceWorld tasks (baseline without history 27.86 vs with history 24.65); eliminating action history improved results, indicating that naively providing the last ten actions (including unsuccessful ones) can introduce noise and degrade Seq2Seq LM decision-making in these environments. However, combining affordances with history (aff ⊕ m) produced the best average reported for Swift (31.31), showing complementary effects when memory is paired with relevant world knowledge.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Raw action-history inclusion can be detrimental (noise / irrelevant/incorrect actions), so content and formatting of history matter; fixed-size history (last 10) may contain many non-informative actions; token budget and input design constrain how much history can be provided; memory that mixes successful and unsuccessful actions may mislead the LM unless paired with additional structured knowledge (e.g., affordances).

extracted_data.name_full: Reflexion agent (ReAct actor with verbal episodic memory) on ALFWorld
extracted_data.brief_description: A language-agent framework that augments an LLM-based Actor (here using ReAct-style prompting) with a long-term episodic memory of verbal self-reflections (natural-language summaries of past failed trajectories) which are appended to the Actor's context to guide future trials; evaluated on the text-based ALFWorld environments.
extracted_data.agent_description: Actor: LLM prompted with ReAct (explicit intermediate thoughts) to generate actions; Evaluator: heuristic or LLM-based binary success signal; Self-Reflection: LLM producing natural-language experience summaries stored as episodic memories (mem) used to condition future action generation.
extracted_data.model_name: GPT-3 (LLM used as Actor / ReAct prompting; unspecified GPT-3 variant in AlfWorld experiments)
extracted_data.memory_type: long-term episodic memory of verbal self-reflections (plus short-term trajectory history as immediate context)
extracted_data.memory_integration: memory entries (self-reflection text) are appended into the Actor's context window (prompt concatenation); short-term trajectory history used as immediate context; mem is a bounded sliding window (Ω typically 1–3) and truncated to last 3 experiences for AlfWorld.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: completed 130/134 tasks (≈97.0% tasks solved) using heuristic self-evaluation and Reflexion; reported absolute improvement of 22 percentage points over the strong baseline across 12 iterative trials.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: ReAct-only baseline (no long-term verbal mem): substantially lower success (paper reports an absolute −22% gap vs Reflexion); baseline converges early (performance increases halt between trials 6 and 7) and exhibits a hallucination rate ≈22%.
extracted_data.key_findings: Persisting natural-language self-reflections stored as long-term episodic memory and concatenated into the LLM prompt enable the agent to (1) identify early mistakes in long trajectories, (2) provide actionable 'self-hints' that improve planning/search in subsequent trials, and (3) substantially boost multi-step text-game performance (∼+22% absolute on ALFWorld) relative to the same LLM acting without the memory.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Memory is constrained by LLM context size (mem window truncated to last Ω entries, typically 1–3); benefits depend on quality of self-evaluation (heuristic or LLM); can get stuck in local minima requiring creative exploration; requires careful bounding to avoid prompt overflow; exact baseline counts not always enumerated for each trial step.

source_info.paper_title: Reflexion: language agents with verbal reinforcement learning

extracted_data.name_full: ReAct + Reflexion agent evaluated on WebShop
extracted_data.brief_description: Same Reflexion framework (LLM Actor with appended verbal self-reflection memory) evaluated on WebShop e-commerce navigation/search tasks; reported failure to improve over baseline in this domain.
extracted_data.agent_description: Actor: LLM using ReAct prompting to interact with a web-based shopping environment; Self-Reflection: LLM-generated verbal summaries of failed trials appended to memory and reused in retries.
extracted_data.model_name: ReAct-style LLM agent (two-shot ReAct + Reflexion; specific LLM not explicitly named for WebShop runs)
extracted_data.memory_type: long-term episodic verbal memory (self-reflections)
extracted_data.memory_integration: self-reflections appended to prompt/context for subsequent trials (same context-concatenation integration as other Reflexion experiments)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: No meaningful improvement reported; ReAct + Reflexion did not significantly outperform ReAct across 100 WebShop customer shopping requests; runs terminated after 4 trials due to no signs of improvement.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: ReAct-only baseline (no long-term verbal mem): similar performance to Reflexion on WebShop (no significant difference reported).
extracted_data.key_findings: Reflexion's verbal episodic memory approach can fail on tasks requiring broad, diverse exploration or highly creative search strategies (e.g., ambiguous e-commerce search); in WebShop experiments the agent did not generate helpful self-reflections and showed no learning improvement within few trials.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Reflexion struggles when task success requires diverse exploration or highly creative/novel strategies not easily recovered by reusing past verbal summaries; self-reflection quality degraded (did not produce helpful guidance), and the approach can get stuck in local minima early (terminated after few trials).

extracted_data.name_full: ThinkThrice (三思) Multi-Agent Framework for Jubensha
extracted_data.brief_description: A multi-agent interaction framework for playing Jubensha (Chinese multi-player detective text games) using LLM-based player agents augmented with a retrieval memory, plus in-context modules for self-refinement and self-verification to improve information gathering and reduce hallucination.
extracted_data.agent_description: An LLM-driven player agent instantiated within the ThinkThrice framework that (1) maintains an external per-agent memory of observations, (2) retrieves recent relevant memories when producing actions, and (3) uses Self-Refinement (SR) to augment initial answers with script facts and Self-Verification (SV) to check facts against the agent's script before outputting answers. Agents operate synchronously in multi-round dialogue under a scripted host.
extracted_data.model_name: GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-16k-0613) for gameplay; GPT-4 (gpt-4-1106-preview) evaluated for some reasoning tasks (both used elsewhere in experiments as noted).
extracted_data.memory_type: Retrieval-augmented external vector memory (per-agent episodic/interaction memory stored as embeddings).
extracted_data.memory_integration: Observations are embedded and stored in an external vector database exclusive to each agent (OpenAI embedding models used). At decision time the agent uses Faiss to retrieve the top-5 most similar historical records and includes those retrieved memory fragments in the prompt (context window) for the LLM.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Jubensha (the authors' new Chinese multi-player scripted murder-mystery text-game dataset / environment)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Factual QA (Other's questions average accuracy): MR only = 0.402; MR+SR = 0.471; MR+SR+SV (N=3 attempts) = 0.498. Similarity (chat history vs all scripts) OpenAI-embedding cosine: MR = 0.798; MR+SR = 0.820; MR+SR+SV (N=3) = 0.831. MR+SR+SV (N=3) achieved the highest average civilian win rate and murderer identification accuracy among tested architectures (exact win-rate numbers not enumerated in table text). In inferential reasoning tasks, MR+SR+SV (N=3) ranked second to Full Script Access; model choice (GPT-4 vs GPT-3.5) further amplified performance.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: No MR (memoryless baseline) — Factual QA: Own-script Q accuracy ≈ 0.770; Other's questions average accuracy = 0.305. Similarity (chat vs scripts) OpenAI-embedding cosine: 0.062. (Reported as the 'No MR' rows in Table 5 and Table 6.)
extracted_data.key_findings: 1) Retrieval-augmented per-agent memory (MR) substantially increases agents' ability to answer factual questions about other players (Other's Q accuracy improves from ~0.305 to ~0.402). 2) Self-Refinement (SR) increases the detail/completeness of responses and further boosts factual accuracy (Other's Q avg up to ~0.471). 3) Self-Verification (SV), allowing multiple regeneration attempts and scoring for authenticity, yields additional gains (MR+SR+SV(N=3) Other's Q avg ≈ 0.498) and produces chat histories more similar to full scripts (OpenAI cosine similarity up to 0.831). 4) Agents with MR+SR+SV achieve the best downstream outcomes (higher civilian win rate and murderer identification accuracy), and more available information (e.g., Full Script Access) yields still stronger inferential performance; model capability (GPT-4 > GPT-3.5) also strongly affects reasoning performance.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Reported issues and limitations include: (a) LLM hallucinations necessitate a verification module (SV) and the SV design still requires attempts and scoring heuristics; (b) limited context windows motivate external memory but introduce design choices (embedding model, chunking, number of retrieved items — authors used top-5); (c) variability and stochasticity of LLM outputs cause experimental variance (authors ran multiple seeds/runs but note variability remains); (d) costs and replication challenges due to proprietary model versions and compute; (e) evaluation primarily in Chinese on the Jubensha dataset, so generalization across languages/games was not established.
extracted_data.supporting_tables_or_figures: Table 5 (factual QA results: No MR vs MR vs MR+SR vs MR+SR+SV), Table 6 (chat/script similarity scores), Figure 3 (win rate and murderer identification accuracy across architectures), Figure 4 and Table 7 (inferential QA and qualitative reasoning comparisons).

extracted_data.name_full: Sweet&Sour (Positive and Negative Experience Reflection with Managed Memory)
extracted_data.brief_description: A reflection method introduced in this paper that augments LLM-based agents by incorporating both positive (sweet) and negative (sour) experiences into self-reflection, together with a managed dual-buffer memory (short-term and long-term) to store reflection tuples for retrieval across attempts.
extracted_data.agent_description: An LLM-based actor agent augmented with an explicit self-reflection module that (1) prompts the agent to verbalize why successful subgoals succeeded (positive experiences) as well as analyze failures, and (2) stores these verbalized reflections in a managed memory for later retrieval to inform subsequent planning.
extracted_data.memory_type: Managed memory (dual-buffer): short-term and long-term episodic-style reflection storage; entries are tuples (reflection_t, o_t, a_t, r_t).
extracted_data.memory_integration: Reflections are stored in short-term while collecting subgoal successes; at attempt end short-term is moved to long-term. Failed-attempt reflections are added immediately to long-term. Stored reflections are provided as additional context for the LLM's self-reflection and action generation across attempts (i.e., retrieved and concatenated into the agent's context).
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Average success scores by underlying LLM — Llama 8B: 32.5/100; Mistral Large 2: 44.6/100; GPT-4o: 54.6/100.
extracted_data.key_findings: Incorporating positive experiences into reflection and using a managed dual-buffer memory leads to substantial performance gains over prior reflection-only (failure-only) methods and baselines, with larger relative improvements for smaller models; Sweet&Sour achieved the highest averages across all evaluated LLMs.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Evaluation limited to a single benchmark (ScienceWorld). Method relies on LLM-generated reflections and thus inherits limitations of LLM reasoning; an ablation that samples only from failures markedly reduces performance, showing sensitivity to the content of stored reflections.

source_info.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

extracted_data.brief_description: An ablation of Sweet&Sour where the agent's reflections are sampled only from failures (i.e., positive-experience reflections excluded), used to test the contribution of positive experiences.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same LLM-based agent and managed memory mechanism as Sweet&Sour but restricted to sampling reflections from failed attempts only (no positive-experience reflections).
extracted_data.memory_type: Managed memory (dual-buffer) but effectively only using failure-derived long-term entries for reflection.
extracted_data.memory_integration: Failure reflections are placed into long-term and used as additional context across attempts; positive short-term entries are not used for sampling in this ablation.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Average success scores (failures-only sampling) — Llama 8B: 24.6/100; Mistral Large 2: 31.1/100; GPT-4o: 44.9/100.
extracted_data.key_findings: Sampling only from failures substantially reduces performance compared to the full Sweet&Sour approach, and yields results similar to prior failure-only reflection methods (evidence that positive-experience reflections materially improve outcomes).
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Shows that excluding positive experiences degrades performance; does not eliminate other limitations of reflection-based methods (e.g., reliance on LLM reasoning and single-environment evaluation).

source_info.paper_title: Positive Experience Reflection for Agents in Interactive Text Environments

extracted_data.name_full: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.brief_description: A prior LLM-based agent that employs self-reflection by verbalizing reasons for failure and storing those reflections in a long-term memory which is used across subsequent attempts to improve performance.
extracted_data.citation_title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.agent_description: An agent built on the ReAct reasoning/acting paradigm that performs self-reflection on unsuccessful attempts, storing failure-derived reflections in a persistent long-term memory across rounds (it iteratively improves across up to four rounds using stored reflections).
extracted_data.model_name: GPT-4o; Mistral Large 2; Llama 3.1 8B (evaluated variants in this study)
extracted_data.memory_type: Long-term memory storing reflections from unsuccessful attempts (failure-only episodic memory).
extracted_data.memory_integration: Stored failure reflections form a persistent history (long-term memory) which is included as additional context for the LLM's reflections and subsequent action selection across attempts (retrieval and context concatenation across rounds).
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Average success scores — Llama 8B: 21.7/100; Mistral Large 2: 27.6/100; GPT-4o: 45.3/100.
extracted_data.key_findings: Reflexion (failure-only reflection + long-term memory) improves over non-reflective baselines on some settings but struggles when agents are initially successful and shows substantially worse efficacy for smaller LLMs compared to Sweet&Sour; it can get 'stuck' (tilt) because it does not reinforce successes.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Focuses only on learning from failures, neglecting positive experiences which limits performance; performance drops markedly for smaller LLMs and on tasks where early successes are not leveraged (reported tendency to 'tilt').

extracted_data.brief_description: An LLM-based agent (Ariadne) that constructs and uses a hybrid memory graph (AriGraph) combining a semantic knowledge graph of extracted triplets and episodic vertices/edges linking observations to those triplets; the graph is incrementally updated from textual observations and retrieved via a two-stage semantic + episodic search for planning and action selection.
extracted_data.agent_description: Pipeline agent with working memory, planning module, and ReAct-based decision-making; long-term memory is the AriGraph world model (semantic vertices/edges + episodic vertices/edges). Observations are parsed by an LLM into triplets to update the semantic graph; episodic vertices store raw observations and episodic edges connect triplets extracted from the same observation. Retrieval uses Contriever embeddings for semantic search (depth d, width w) and an episodic ranking function to return top-k episodic memories; retrieved content is placed into working memory for planning and action selection.
extracted_data.model_name: gpt-4-0125-preview (TextWorld experiments); GPT-4 / GPT-4o variants in some evaluations
extracted_data.memory_type: Hybrid: semantic knowledge graph (structured) + episodic memory (raw observations linked to triplets)
extracted_data.memory_integration: External structured graph (AriGraph) updated online from extracted triplets; retrieval pipeline (semantic search via Contriever + graph expansion, then episodic search returning relevant episodic vertices) injects results into the agent's working memory / prompt for planning and ReAct decision-making; also supplies graph-based navigation actions (e.g. 'go to location').
extracted_data.benchmark_name: TextWorld (Treasure Hunt, Cleaning, Cooking) and NetHack (restricted observations)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: TextWorld normalized scores (average of top-3 runs): Treasure Hunt: 1.0 (complete), Treasure Hunt Hard: 1.0, Treasure Hunt Hardest: 1.0, Cooking: 1.0, Cooking Hard: 1.0, Cooking Hardest: 0.65, Cleaning: 0.79; NetHack (Room obs): score 593.00 ± 202.62, levels 6.33 ± 2.31.
extracted_data.key_findings: Structured hybrid memory (AriGraph) substantially improves LLM agent performance in partially observable text games: Ariadne with AriGraph outperforms full-history, summarization, RAG, Simulacra, Reflexion baselines and strong RL baselines across tasks, scales to harder environments, and achieves near top-human performance in several tasks. Episodic memory is critical for tasks requiring recall of detailed instructions (e.g., Cooking), while semantic graph supports navigation and state tracking.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Relies on accurate triplet extraction by the LLM; graph construction quality depends on LLM backbone (growth rate decreases with higher-quality LLMs); token usage and prompt sizes are non-trivial (Ariadne prompt ~6,000 tokens per step); approach currently text-only, not multimodal; some task-specific weaknesses (e.g., Cleaning shows lower performance relative to best human players), and complexity of graph updates/consistency management.

source_info.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: Ariadne agent variant using the semantic knowledge graph but with episodic vertices/edges disabled, to test the contribution of episodic memory.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same cognitive architecture and semantic graph updates, but episodic vertices/edges are omitted; retrieval relies only on semantic triplets.
extracted_data.memory_type: Semantic knowledge graph only (no episodic memory)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Semantic graph retrieval injected into working memory; no episodic search or episodic raw-observation recall.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized scores (from ablation table): Treasure Hunt: 1.0, Treasure Hunt Hard: 0.67, Cooking: 0.64, Cooking Hard: 0.45, Cleaning: 0.92 (note differing effects depending on task).
extracted_data.key_findings: Removing episodic memory degrades performance on tasks that require recalling detailed past observations (notably Cooking and Treasure Hunt Hard), demonstrating episodic memory's importance for procedural and instruction-heavy tasks; interestingly, for Cleaning the semantic-only variant performed well, highlighting task-dependent roles of memory types.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Semantic-only graphs may omit fine-grained temporal or contextual details stored in episodic memories, causing failures on tasks that require precise recall of past observations or sequences of events.

source_info.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

extracted_data.name_full: Full history memory baseline (complete record of observations/actions)
extracted_data.brief_description: Baseline in which the agent retains the complete sequence of past observations and actions and includes them in the prompt/context for decision-making.
extracted_data.agent_description: Ariadne planning and decision-making modules are used, but the long-term memory is implemented by concatenating the full history of observations and actions into the prompt/context window.
extracted_data.memory_type: Full context / full history
extracted_data.memory_integration: Context window concatenation: entire episode history is appended to prompt/working memory each step.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized scores (Table 4): Treasure Hunt: 0.47, Cooking: 0.18, Cleaning: 0.05.
extracted_data.key_findings: Retaining full history is inefficient and insufficient: Full-history agents perform poorly relative to AriGraph and often fail to scale to more complex/harder variants (heavy token costs and limited reasoning over unstructured long contexts).
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: High token usage (Full history ~14,000 prompt tokens by step 150), inefficient retrieval, difficulty in extracting structured relations and planning from long unstructured context.

source_info.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: Baseline that compresses history into summaries iteratively, reducing context size while attempting to preserve salient information.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same agent pipeline but uses iterative summarization to compress past observations into shorter summaries stored in memory and included in prompts.
extracted_data.memory_type: Summarized memory / compressed history
extracted_data.memory_integration: Summaries stored externally and concatenated into working memory/prompt; iterative update of summaries as new observations arrive.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized scores (Table 4): Treasure Hunt: 0.33, Treasure Hunt Hard: 0.17, Cooking: 0.52, Cooking Hard: 0.21, Cleaning: 0.35.
extracted_data.key_findings: Summarization reduces token usage but loses structure and fine-grained details; performs better than full-history in some tasks (e.g., Cooking medium) but is outperformed by AriGraph, especially on tasks needing structured relations and multi-step planning.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Summaries may discard critical localized facts needed for planning; selection of what to summarize is nontrivial and can harm performance in partially observable tasks.

source_info.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: Baseline using vector retrieval over an external memory index to augment model prompts with k most similar past memories to the current query/observation.
extracted_data.agent_description: Ariadne planning and decision-making are used; memory is an external vector store of past observations/triplets and retrieval returns top-k similar items to augment the prompt.
extracted_data.memory_type: Retrieval-augmented memory (vector store)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Embedding-based retrieval (top-k) from external DB; retrieved items are appended to working memory/prompt.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized scores (Table 4): Treasure Hunt: 0.33, Treasure Hunt Hard: 0.17, Cooking: 0.36, Cooking Hard: 0.17, Cleaning: 0.39.
extracted_data.key_findings: Standard RAG offers improvements over naive baselines in some cases but suffers from unstructured nature: related facts can be scattered and not linked, limiting multi-hop reasoning and planning compared to AriGraph's structured retrieval.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Unstructured retrieval can miss related facts scattered across memory; lack of explicit relations hampers planning/navigation in POMDPs.

source_info.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

extracted_data.name_full: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.brief_description: Baseline where the agent reflects on failed trials to generate 'reflections' that are stored and used across subsequent trials to improve future performance.
extracted_data.citation_title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.agent_description: Agent stores trial-level reflections produced after failures and uses them as additional memory between trials; differs from other methods by explicitly generating reinforcement-learning style reflections across episodes.
extracted_data.memory_type: Inter-episode reflective memory / episodic reflections
extracted_data.memory_integration: Reflections are appended to memory between trials and used in prompts for subsequent trials; operates across multiple trial runs (multi-trial memory).
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized scores (Table 4): Treasure Hunt: 0.93, Cooking: 1.0, Cleaning: 0.27 (not all entries reported for hard variants).
extracted_data.key_findings: Reflexion can provide large gains when multi-trial reflection is applicable (e.g., strong immediate gains on some tasks), but performance can degrade across subsequent tries for some tasks; still outperformed by AriGraph in single-episode interactive settings due to lack of structured knowledge and per-step retrieval.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Requires multiple trial runs to benefit (reflection across episodes), less suitable for single-episode or single-run tasks; lacks structural semantic memory for navigation and multi-hop retrieval.

source_info.paper_title: AriGraph: Learning Knowledge Graph World Models with Episodic Memory for LLM Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: Memory implementation inspired by Generative Agents (Park et al., 2023) that stores episodic notes and scores them by recency/importance/relevance and uses these during decision-making.
extracted_data.citation_title: Generative agents: Interactive simulacra of human behavior
extracted_data.agent_description: Uses a scoring mechanism combining recency, importance, relevance, and reflections to select and include memory snippets into the prompt; implemented as an alternative memory module for Ariadne.
extracted_data.memory_type: Episodic memory with importance/recency scoring
extracted_data.memory_integration: Score-based retrieval of episodic memory entries; retrieved entries are injected into the prompt/working memory.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized scores (Table 4): Treasure Hunt: 0.40, Cooking: 0.30, Cleaning: 0.70.
extracted_data.key_findings: Simulacra-style episodic memory helps in some tasks (e.g., Cleaning) due to emphasis on recency and importance, but lacks structured semantic relations; AriGraph's joint semantic+episodic graph offers more robust multi-step planning and navigation.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Primarily stores unstructured snippets scored heuristically — can miss cross-observation multi-hop relations and spatial/topological information required for navigation.

extracted_data.brief_description: An LLM-based NetHack agent used as a baseline; 'Level obs' provides a memory oracle with full explored-level information, while 'Room obs' restricts observations to the current room, serving to test the AriGraph's ability to compensate via memory.

extracted_data.memory_type: Observation-level memory/oracle (Level obs) vs. no memory (Room obs) depending on configuration

extracted_data.name_full: Monte Carlo planning with Dynamic Memory-guided Large language model
extracted_data.brief_description: An MCTS-based planning algorithm that uses an LLM as a prior policy and augments it with two memory mechanisms—in-trial (short-term trajectory context) and cross-trial (episodic reflections)—to dynamically adjust action priors during search.
extracted_data.agent_description: An MCTS agent which replaces the usual learned policy prior with an LLM policy conditioned on in-trial memory (recent trajectory window) and cross-trial memory (stored reflections from past failed trajectories). The LLM returns a probability distribution over valid actions which is used as the prior term in a PUCT-style action selection.
extracted_data.memory_type: in-trial short-term trajectory context and cross-trial episodic (reflection) memory
extracted_data.memory_integration: Both memory types are concatenated into the LLM prompt (context window). The LLM produces action token probabilities conditioned on in-trial memory Mi and cross-trial memory Mc; Mc stores up to k=3 reflections per root and is appended to prompts for subsequent simulations to alter action priors used inside PUCT (see Eq. 3).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Jericho (9 text-based games: Zork1, Deephome, Ludicorp, Pentari, Detective, Library, Balances, Temple, Ztuu)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Per-game scores with full MC-DML (average over 3 runs where reported): Zork1: 48.66 ± 1.89; Deephome: 67 ± 1.41; Ludicorp: 19.67 ± 1.7; Pentari: 70.0; Detective: 346.67 ± 9.43; Library: 21.0; Balances: 10.0; Temple: 8.0; Ztuu: 23.67 ± 1.9.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Ablation (without cross-trial memory w.o. Mc) reported on subset: Zork1: 38.33 ± 2.89; Deephome: 62.66 ± 0.94; Detective: 326.67 ± 4.71; Ztuu: 20.66 ± 0.47. Further ablations removing both Mc and Mi show larger drops (e.g., Zork1 down to 31.67 ± 4.7).
extracted_data.key_findings: Augmenting an LLM prior with both in-trial (recent trajectory) and cross-trial (reflections from failures) memory substantially improves initial planning performance in text-based games; MC-DML outperforms pure LLM agents, reflection-only baselines, and matches or exceeds MCTS+RL baselines at their early planning iteration (notably strong on Deephome and Zork1). Ablations show both memory types are important, with cross-trial reflection giving large gains in avoiding bottlenecks.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: In-trial memory is implemented as a short trajectory window and may miss clues encountered far earlier (‘needle in a haystack’ issue); input window constraints require truncation/compression. Cross-trial memory is limited (k=3 reflections) in experiments. The approach requires many LLM calls (cost/latency) and relies on accurate token log-probabilities from the LLM backend.

extracted_data.name_full: Reflection-based LLM agent (as implemented in this paper)
extracted_data.brief_description: A baseline agent that lets an LLM generate reflections after completed rounds (failed trajectories) and re-uses those reflections as cross-trial memory to guide the LLM in subsequent rounds.
extracted_data.citation_title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.agent_description: An LLM-based gameplay agent that collects short textual reflections at the end of episodes and supplies them as persistent memory for future episodes; in this paper the reflection mechanism follows Shinn et al. (2024) and is used as a baseline to compare with MC-DML.
extracted_data.memory_type: cross-trial episodic reflection memory
extracted_data.memory_integration: Reflections are generated after failed gameplay trajectories and concatenated into the LLM prompt for subsequent episodes (cross-trial prompt augmentation); in experiments reflections were limited to up to 3.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Jericho (used as baseline across same 9 games)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Reflection baseline per Table 2: Zork1: 5; Deephome: 1; Ludicorp: 4; Pentari: 5; Detective: 30; Library: 6; Balances: 10; Temple: 8; Ztuu: 5.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: LLM-only baseline (no reflection) per Table 2: Zork1: 0; Deephome: 1; Ludicorp: 4; Pentari: 5; Detective: 30; Library: 6; Balances: 10; Temple: 8; Ztuu: 0.
extracted_data.key_findings: Simple reflection (cross-trial textual feedback) yields small improvements over an LLM-only direct agent in some settings but is far less effective than MC-DML's combined in-trial + cross-trial integration inside MCTS; reflection alone does not solve exploration/exploitation balance and often results in limited overall gains.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Limited number of reflections (experiments capped at 3) and modest improvements; reflection-only agents still suffer from looped behavior and poor exploration; improvements depend on how reflections are incorporated and on the environment complexity.

extracted_data.brief_description: Prior work that introduced an LLM agent which generates textual reflections from failed trajectories and reuses them as a form of episodic memory to improve future decisions.
extracted_data.citation_title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.agent_description: A language-agent paradigm where the LLM produces short reflective summaries of failed episodes (verbal reinforcement) that are then used as memory/heuristics for future rounds, enabling iterative improvement without explicit retraining.
extracted_data.memory_type: episodic reflection memory (cross-trial)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Reflections (natural-language suggestions) are stored and included in later prompts to guide the LLM's action generation; treated as interpretable episodic memory.
extracted_data.key_findings: Cited as inspiration for cross-trial memory; demonstrates that verbal reflections can be used as persistent episodic memory to guide LLM agents across trials.

extracted_data.brief_description: A previously proposed method that uses a large language model as a fixed prior policy inside MCTS to provide commonsense priors for action selection, but without mechanisms to learn from past failures.
extracted_data.citation_title: Large language models as commonsense knowledge for large-scale task planning
extracted_data.agent_description: MCTS algorithm that uses an LLM to provide a prior action distribution for PUCT but treats the LLM as a fixed, non-updating prior (no cross-trial reflection or memory-driven updating).

extracted_data.brief_description: A transformer-based RL agent that learns and maintains a continuous, latent multi-relational belief graph as an explicit structured memory from raw text observations, and uses this graph (encoded by an R-GCN) together with text via bi-directional attention to select actions in text-based games.
extracted_data.agent_description: Learns a continuous adjacency-tensor belief graph G_t (dimensions: relations x entities x entities) via a recurrent graph-updater (Δg_t -> RNN -> decode to G_t). Graph is encoded with an R-GCN (conditioned on relation label embeddings) and fused with transformer text encodings via a bi-attention aggregator; action scoring uses self-attention + MLP. Graph-updater is pre-trained with self-supervised Observation Generation (OG) and Contrastive Observation Classification (COC), then frozen while the action selector is trained with Double DQN variants.
extracted_data.model_name: Transformer-based (custom encoders/decoders); R-GCN for graph encoding
extracted_data.memory_type: Continuous latent belief graph (structured graph memory)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Recurrent hidden state (RNN) decodes to adjacency tensor G_t (graph memory); G_t is encoded with R-GCN and fused with text via bi-directional attention; used as input to the action scorer.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: TextWorld (500+ generated games across multiple difficulty levels)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Example (best reported GATA variant: COC pre-trained + observation input): normalized test scores for 100-training-game setting by difficulty levels 1–5: [62.5, 33.0, 46.7, 25.9, 33.4] (Table 2). Reported average relative improvement over the Tr-DQN text baseline: +24.2% (paper-average figure).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Text-only transformer baselines (Tr-DQN): normalized test scores for 100-training-game setting by difficulty levels 1–5: [62.5, 32.0, 38.3, 17.7, 34.6]. (These are the main text-only baselines used for comparison.)
extracted_data.key_findings: Learning an explicit, structured graph belief as memory helps planning and generalization: GATA (continuous learned belief graphs) outperforms text-only transformer baselines (Tr-DQN/Tr-DRQN variants) on unseen TextWorld games, delivering substantial average relative improvements (paper reports ≈+20–24% improvements depending on variant/setting). Including immediate text observations with the belief graph further improves performance via attention.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Belief graphs are dense/noisy (not sparse/fully interpretable) because learned unsupervised from text; accuracy gap remains relative to full-state (ground-truth) graphs. Learning the graph updater requires careful self-supervised pretraining; errors in inferred graphs can accumulate and affect downstream performance.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

extracted_data.brief_description: A GATA variant that uses the environment's ground-truth full graph (complete discrete game state) as the agent's memory input to the action selector (no graph-updater), representing an upper bound for graph-based memory performance.
extracted_data.agent_description: Action selector identical to GATA but instead of using an inferred belief graph it directly receives the ground-truth full graph G^{full} from TextWorld as input; no graph-updater module is used.
extracted_data.memory_type: Ground-truth full graph (perfect explicit state memory)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Direct input of ground-truth KG to graph encoder (R-GCN) and aggregation with text; no updater/recurrent inference needed.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized test scores for 100-training-game setting by difficulty levels 1–5: [95.0, 95.0, 70.0, 37.3, 52.8] (Table 2). Reported average relative improvement over Tr-DQN baseline: +81.6% (serves as an empirical upper bound).
extracted_data.key_findings: Feeding a perfect, fully-observable ground-truth graph yields substantially higher performance than inferred belief graphs or text-only baselines, confirming that structured memory that contains complete state information greatly helps policy learning.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: This setting relaxes partial observability and is not available in realistic scenarios; it mainly functions as an upper bound to evaluate potential of structured graph memories.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

extracted_data.brief_description: A variant of GATA whose graph-updater is pre-trained on ground-truth discrete KGs (from FTWP) and which maintains a discrete belief graph using add/delete update commands produced by a seq2seq generator.
extracted_data.agent_description: Uses a transformer seq2seq 'command generation' discrete graph updater that emits add(node1,node2,relation) and delete(...) operations to update a discrete belief graph; the resulting discrete graph is encoded by the same R-GCN-based action selector as GATA.
extracted_data.memory_type: Discrete belief graph (explicit symbolic graph memory)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Seq2Seq-generated discrete update operations (add/delete) are applied to the discrete belief graph; the graph is encoded by R-GCN and used by the scorer.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Example (reported): normalized test scores for 100-training-game setting (one reported variant) by difficulty levels 1–5: [62.5, 32.0, 51.7, 21.8, 23.5] (Table 2). Paper reports mixed results: GATA-GTP can outperform text baselines in some settings (especially when combined with text observations) but overall performs worse than continuous GATA variants.
extracted_data.key_findings: Pre-training the discrete updater on ground-truth graphs yields a discrete symbolic memory that can help, but the discrete updater is vulnerable to accumulated errors and discretization effects; overall, continuous learned belief graphs (GATA) often outperform this discrete-updater variant.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Discrete update operations and discrete graph representations tend to accumulate errors over time and suffer from brittleness/round-off issues; discrete generation can be harder to recover from mistakes compared to continuous belief-graph updates.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

extracted_data.brief_description: A transformer-based variant of DRQN (recurrent DQN) implemented as a text-only baseline in this work; it provides an implicit recurrent memory via an RNN component in the policy.
extracted_data.agent_description: Transformer text encoder combined with a recurrent policy (DRQN-style) to provide an implicit memory via recurrent hidden states; trained with RL (Double DQN, prioritized replay, etc.) as a text-only baseline.
extracted_data.memory_type: Implicit recurrent memory (RNN hidden state)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Recurrent hidden state maintained across timesteps (DRQN) provides memory for partially observable state; used directly by action scoring.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized test scores for 100-training-game setting by difficulty levels 1–5: [58.8, 31.0, 36.7, 21.4, 27.4] (Table 2). Reported relative improvement over Tr-DQN baseline (text-only non-recurrent): modest (paper reports Tr-DRQN outperforms Tr-DQN by a small margin, ~+3.9% average in one summary).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Tr-DQN (transformer text-only, no recurrent memory): [62.5, 32.0, 38.3, 17.7, 34.6] for 100-training-games (Table 2).
extracted_data.key_findings: An implicit RNN memory (DRQN) can improve performance relative to a non-recurrent text-only agent in some settings, indicating that a learned recurrent memory helps with partial observability; however, GATA's explicit structured memory yields larger gains in generalization and reward optimization.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: DRQN baselines struggle to scale on larger/more diverse training sets compared to GATA; implicit recurrent memory is less structured and provides weaker inductive bias than explicit graph memories.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

extracted_data.name_full: Transformer-based DRQN with Episodic Counting Bonus
extracted_data.brief_description: A transformer + recurrent baseline augmented with a count-based exploration bonus (episodic counting) to encourage exploration and act as a form of memory-informed exploration.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same as Tr-DRQN but augmented with an episodic counting bonus (as in prior work) to encourage exploration; implemented as a baseline for comparison to GATA.
extracted_data.model_name: Transformer encoder + recurrent policy (DRQN) with count-based exploration bonus
extracted_data.memory_type: Implicit recurrent memory + episodic count-based exploration memory
extracted_data.memory_integration: Recurrent hidden state for temporal memory; episodic counts computed to shape exploration reward.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Normalized test scores for 100-training-game setting by difficulty levels 1–5: [58.8, 33.0, 33.3, 19.5, 30.6] (Table 2). Paper reports small/modest improvements over Tr-DQN in some regimes but overall inferior to GATA.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Tr-DQN (transformer text-only baseline without recurrent memory or counting): [62.5, 32.0, 38.3, 17.7, 34.6] for 100-training-games.
extracted_data.key_findings: Episodic counting as an auxiliary exploration/memory mechanism helps exploration but does not close the gap with structured graph memories; Tr-DRQN+ shows modest changes compared to Tr-DRQN/Tr-DQN and is outperformed by GATA.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Count-based exploration bonuses are an indirect form of memory/exploration shaping and may not provide the structured relational information that graph memories supply; they also do not eliminate partial-observability limitations.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs to Generalize on Text-Based Games

extracted_data.name_full: Count-based memory for exploration in text-based games
extracted_data.brief_description: A prior approach that uses episodic counting bonuses as a memory/exploration mechanism to encourage coverage and generalization across text-game states; cited in this paper as a prior baseline for memory-augmented agents.
extracted_data.citation_title: Counting to explore and generalize in text-based games
extracted_data.agent_description: Uses state/observation counting to compute exploration bonuses (episodic counts) to guide exploration and implicitly act as a short-term memory/novelty signal for RL agents in text games.
extracted_data.memory_type: Episodic count-based memory / exploration bonus
extracted_data.memory_integration: Counts computed from visited states/observations are used to augment reward during RL (exploration shaping), rather than as an explicit structured memory fed into the policy.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: TextWorld (used as prior baseline in cited works)
extracted_data.key_findings: Mentioned as a promising exploration/memory mechanism that can improve generalization and exploration in text games, but is conceptually different from explicit structured graph memories; used as a baseline in the paper's comparisons.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Counting bonuses provide only a novelty signal and do not encode relational state structure; they may be insufficient for tasks that require remembering relations or attributes across long horizons.

extracted_data.name_full: Memory networks / ranking systems for adventure-style dialog and acting
extracted_data.brief_description: Prior work using memory networks and ranking approaches to tackle interactive/adventure dialog tasks; cited in this paper as related memory-augmented language agents for text environments.
extracted_data.citation_title: Learning to speak and act in a fantasy text adventure game
extracted_data.agent_description: Models that use explicit memory architectures (memory networks) and ranking mechanisms to combine past dialog/context with current inputs to select next dialog/action in interactive text tasks.
extracted_data.memory_type: External memory (neural memory network)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Memory network modules that attend over stored context/memory entries and combine with current input representations for decision making.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Interactive fiction / text adventure tasks (as in referenced work)
extracted_data.key_findings: Referenced as an example of prior memory-augmented language agents for interactive fiction; these systems show the value of explicit memory modules for language-based interaction tasks.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Prior memory network approaches are not directly comparable in this paper's experimental setup; they may require different supervision or heuristics.

extracted_data.brief_description: A framework that augments LLM agents with an external episodic memory of past successful task executions (plans, actions, observations) and retrieves contextually-relevant trajectory windows to provide in-context examples for action generation.
extracted_data.agent_description: Modular LLM agent composed of Reasoner (generates overall plan, action plans, and retrieval keys), Retriever (computes weighted similarity to memory logs and returns trajectory windows), and Executor (uses retrieved trajectory windows as in-context examples to generate next actions). Designed for text-only and multimodal environments.
extracted_data.memory_type: Retrieval-augmented episodic memory (external logs of successful task executions: task info, overall plans, action-observation trajectories; multimodal observations supported)
extracted_data.memory_integration: External memory database → Retriever computes weighted similarity (task / overall plan / retrieval-key congruence) using embeddings (sentence-transformers for text, CLIP-based ViT for images) → returns a windowed trajectory around most-similar action → Executor inserts retrieved trajectory windows into prompt as in-context examples for LLM action generation.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: ALFWorld (GPT-3.5, RAP): success rate 85.8% (All); RAP_train (memory built from training set): 91.0%; RAP (GPT-4): 94.8% overall success; RAP (Llama2-13b): 53.0% overall success. WebShop (GPT-3.5, RAP): success rate 48.0%, overall score 76.1%; WebShop (Llama2-13b, RAP): success rate 36.0%, score 71.1%.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: ALFWorld ReAct (GPT-3.5 baseline): 52.2% success; ReAct (GPT-4): 85.8%; ReAct (Llama2-13b): 36.6%. WebShop ReAct (GPT-3.5): success 35.0%, score 61.8%; ReAct (Llama2-13b): success 31.0%, score 64.6%.
extracted_data.key_findings: Retrieval-augmented episodic memory (RAP) substantially improves text-game performance versus baseline agents that do not retrieve prior successful trajectories: large gains reported on ALFWorld and WebShop across multiple LLMs. Memory constructed by one model (GPT-3.5) transfers to another model (Llama2-13b). Retrieval can be switched between action-based and observation-based keys, and using image-based retrieval via CLIP can yield slight improvements over text-only retrieval.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Requires careful retrieval-design (weighted similarity across task/plan/key, environment-specific weight calibration); full trajectories can introduce noise so RAP uses windowed trajectory excerpts; performance depends on quality of embeddings (sentence-transformers / CLIP) and the set of stored successful episodes (only successful episodes are stored); paper does not report limits on memory scale or retrieval latency in deployment.

source_info.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: An agent paradigm that interleaves chain-of-thought style reasoning and environment actions, used as a baseline for sequential decision-making in text environments.
extracted_data.citation_title: ReAct: Synergizing reasoning and acting in language models
extracted_data.agent_description: LLM-based agent that alternates explicit reasoning (thoughts) and actions to interact with environments; does not augment with external episodic memory for retrieval of past task trajectories in the experiments reported here.
extracted_data.model_name: GPT-3.5, GPT-4, Llama2-13b (evaluated as baselines in this paper)
extracted_data.memory_type: none (baseline; uses current context and LLM in-context reasoning only)
extracted_data.memory_integration: N/A (no external retrieval-augmented memory integrated in baseline runs)
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: ALFWorld: ReAct (GPT-3.5) overall success 52.2%; ReAct (GPT-4) 85.8%; ReAct (Llama2-13b) 36.6%. WebShop: ReAct (GPT-3.5) success 35.0%, score 61.8%; ReAct (Llama2-13b) success 31.0%, score 64.6%.
extracted_data.key_findings: Used as the principal baseline: RAP shows substantial improvements over ReAct on ALFWorld and WebShop when RAP supplies retrieved prior successful trajectories as in-context examples.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Without external episodic memory, ReAct can fail to leverage analogies from prior successful episodes and shows lower success rates on multi-step text tasks in this paper's benchmarks.

source_info.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

extracted_data.name_full: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.brief_description: A method that has agents self-reflect on failures to iteratively improve performance by analyzing unsuccessful episodes and generating textual reflections to guide future attempts.
extracted_data.citation_title: Reflexion: Language agents with verbal reinforcement learning
extracted_data.agent_description: Approach that requires LLMs to self-reflect on unsuccessful tasks (verbal reflections) to improve solving future instances; discussed as prior work and included in comparative tables (reported numbers taken from the Reflexion paper).
extracted_data.memory_type: Textual self-reflection / experience logging (verbal reflections of past failures)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Textual reflections generated by LLMs on failed attempts are used for subsequent improvement (paper describes reflection loop rather than retrieval of episodic trajectory windows).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: ALFWorld, WebShop (reported comparisons in paper tables)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: ALFWorld (reported, GPT-3.5): overall success 74.6%. WebShop (reported, GPT-3.5): success 35.0%, score 61.8%.
extracted_data.key_findings: Cited as a prior-memory-like method; Reflexion's self-reflection approach yields improvements over simple baselines in prior work and is used as a comparative reference point in tables; RAP outperforms Reflexion on ALFWorld in the paper's reported comparisons.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Paper notes Reflexion focuses on reflecting on trajectories within the same task and may be restricted in generalizing experiences across diverse tasks; authors contrast RAP's ability to implicitly draw from a broader set of past experiences without an explicit extra reflection step.

source_info.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

extracted_data.name_full: ADaPT: As-needed decomposition and planning with language models
extracted_data.brief_description: An approach that decomposes tasks into sub-tasks and re-executes where necessary to improve performance; referenced and compared by reported metrics in the paper.
extracted_data.citation_title: Adapt: As-needed decomposition and planning with language models
extracted_data.agent_description: Method that dynamically decomposes tasks and re-plans subcomponents to improve execution; included in comparative tables via reported performance from the ADaPT paper.
extracted_data.memory_type: Task decomposition and re-execution (not a retrieval-augmented episodic memory in the RAP sense)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Decomposition and re-execution procedure (reported results taken from prior work; no external memory retrieval integrated as RAP does).
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: ALFWorld (reported, GPT-3.5): overall success 71.6%. WebShop (reported, GPT-3.5): success 43.0%, score 64.0%.
extracted_data.key_findings: ADaPT is a prior planning/decomposition approach; RAP outperforms ADaPT on ALFWorld and WebShop in the comparisons reported in this paper.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Paper positions ADaPT as limited to task-specific re-execution patterns, whereas RAP emphasizes retrieving diverse successful trajectories across tasks for analogical in-context learning.

source_info.paper_title: RAP: Retrieval-Augmented Planning with Contextual Memory for Multimodal LLM Agents

extracted_data.name_full: ExpeL: LLM Agents Are Experiential Learners
extracted_data.brief_description: A prior method that extracts insights from experiences by passing generated experiences into the LLM to reflect in a text-based manner, aiming to enhance learning of language agents.
extracted_data.citation_title: ExpeL: LLM Agents Are Experiential Learners
extracted_data.agent_description: Approach that collects and feeds generated experiences into the LLM to produce textual reflections/insights for improving future behavior; discussed in related work and contrasted with RAP's implicit retrieval approach.
extracted_data.memory_type: Textual experience storage and reflection (experience database with textual processing)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Feeds all generated experiences into the LLM to reflect in text (explicit reflection step), rather than implicitly retrieving relevant past trajectories as in RAP.
extracted_data.key_findings: Cited as related work that uses explicit textual reflection on stored experiences; RAP differs by retrieving many varied successful episodes and presenting them as in-context examples without requiring an explicit re-tasking/reflection step.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Paper notes ExpeL's approach passes all experiences into the LLM for reflection, which may be less selective than RAP's similarity-weighted retrieval and windowing; no direct numeric comparisons in this paper.

extracted_data.name_full: Behavior Cloned Transformer augmented with Neurosymbolic Symbolic Modules
extracted_data.brief_description: A T5-base behavior-cloned transformer agent fine-tuned on oracle (gold) trajectories and augmented at inference time with external symbolic modules (calculator, navigation/map reader, sorting, and a knowledge-base) whose actions are injected into the environment's valid action space.
extracted_data.agent_description: A behavior-cloned sequence-to-sequence agent based on T5-base that takes task description, current observation, previous action and previous observation as input and generates the next action; during both training and evaluation the agent receives the environment's valid action list and, when augmented, the valid actions produced by symbolic modules are concatenated into that action list so the model can choose module actions.
extracted_data.memory_type: external symbolic modules / structured external memory (knowledge base, navigation map, calculator, sorted lists)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Module actions are injected into the environment's valid-action set (A_t <- A_t^e ∪ A_t^m). Modules monitor observations and enumerate module-specific actions; if the agent selects a module action, that module produces the next observation/result (i.e., modules act as external tool-like memory/KB accessed via action selection).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: MapReader, Arithmetic, Sorting, Text World Common Sense (TWC)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: MapReader: score 1.00 (steps 10); Arithmetic: score 1.00 (steps 5); Sorting: score 0.98 (steps 8); TWC: score 0.97 (steps 3); Average: score 0.99 (avg steps 7). (Scores normalized 0–1.)
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: MapReader: score 0.71 (steps 27); Arithmetic: score 0.56 (steps 5); Sorting: score 0.72 (steps 7); TWC: score 0.90 (steps 6); Average: score 0.72 (avg steps 11).
extracted_data.key_findings: Injecting symbolic module actions into the action space enables the behavior-cloned T5 agent to nearly solve all four evaluated pick-and-place text-game benchmarks, improving average normalized score from 0.72 to 0.99 and reducing average steps from 11 to 7; modules provide accurate external reasoning (arithmetic, map shortest-path, sorting, KB lookups) that the transformer learns to exploit when trained with gold trajectories.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Requires privileged aids: (1) gold trajectories demonstrating module usage for training the BCT (behavior cloning); (2) an exhaustive valid-action list (valid action aid) provided at each step; symbolic modules can greatly expand the action space (e.g. ~530 KB actions for TWC), increasing combinatorial complexity; results rely on the ability of the model to match generated text to valid actions (beam search + string-overlap/cosine heuristics).

source_info.paper_title: Behavior Cloned Transformers are Neurosymbolic Reasoners

extracted_data.name_full: Deep Reinforcement Relevance Network with injected Symbolic Module actions
extracted_data.brief_description: A DRRN reinforcement-learning agent (GRU encoders + Q-network) evaluated with symbolic-module actions added to the environment's valid-action set; despite access to the same module actions, the DRRN does not benefit and performance can decrease.
extracted_data.agent_description: Deep Reinforcement Relevance Network that separately encodes observations and candidate actions with GRUs and predicts Q-values for (observation, candidate action) pairs; used as a baseline RL agent in the same text-game benchmarks, trained for 100k steps across parallel environments.
extracted_data.model_name: DRRN (GRU-based Q-network) — model described in He et al. (2016)
extracted_data.memory_type: external symbolic modules / structured external memory (same modules as for BCT injected into action space)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Module actions are enumerated and concatenated into the environment's valid-action list; the DRRN treats these as candidate actions and ranks them by Q-value (no separate retrieval mechanism beyond treating module actions as ordinary candidate actions).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: MapReader, Arithmetic, Sorting, Text World Common Sense (TWC)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Average (with modules): score 0.14 (avg steps 27). Per-benchmark (with modules): MapReader 0.02 (steps 50), Arithmetic 0.14 (steps 7), Sorting 0.03 (steps 18), TWC 0.37 (steps 34).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Average (no modules): score 0.20 (avg steps 27). Per-benchmark (no modules): MapReader 0.02 (steps 50), Arithmetic 0.17 (steps 10), Sorting 0.03 (steps 21), TWC 0.57 (steps 27).
extracted_data.key_findings: Adding symbolic-module actions into the action space did not improve DRRN performance on these tasks; on average performance decreased (0.20 -> 0.14) and efficiency did not improve. The authors hypothesize the DRRN struggles because it favors actions yielding immediate reward without executing prerequisite steps (e.g., reading the math problem), and because the enlarged action spaces make RL exploration and credit assignment harder.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: DRRN's training signal is ungeneralizable when agents select reward-yielding actions prematurely; large and expanded action spaces (modules add many candidate actions) create exploration and combinatorial trajectory challenges; no specialized mechanism (e.g., training-time demonstrations) was provided to teach the DRRN to exploit module actions.
