extracted_data.brief_description: A multi-task transformer-based world model that uses an explicit knowledge-graph memory to predict graph differences between steps and generate contextually valid natural-language actions; it encodes observations and graphs with BERT-like encoders and decodes with GPT-2-like autoregressive decoders trained with a Set-of-Sequences loss.
extracted_data.agent_description: Multi-task architecture: BERT-like text encoder (pretrained, fine-tuned), BERT-like graph encoder (pretrained on triples), an aggregator, and two GPT-2-like autoregressive decoders (one for graph-diff generation, one for valid-action generation). Trains jointly to predict G_{t+1}-G_t and V_{t+1} using a Set-of-Sequences (SOS) loss and predicts graph diffs rather than full graphs.
extracted_data.memory_type: Structured knowledge graph (explicit symbolic triples); memory represented as G_t and updated via predicted graph differences (G_{t+1}-G_t).
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph G_t is encoded by a graph encoder (BERT-style, phrase-level masking pretraining) into a vector representation G_t; combined with observation encoding O_t in an aggregator to form S_t; decoders condition on S_t and use cross-attention to G_t or O_t as appropriate. The model explicitly predicts graph-diffs to update the memory.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: JerichoWorld (27 training games, 9 test games drawn from Jericho text games)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Knowledge-graph prediction (overall size-weighted): graph-level EM 39.15 (significantly best), graph-level F1 41.06; token-level EM 51.32, token-level F1 52.45. Valid-action prediction (overall): EM 23.22, F1 25.54.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Ablation equivalent to Seq2Seq (no graph-diff, no multi-task, no SOS): knowledge-graph prediction overall graph EM 14.29, F1 15.54 (token EM 18.80, F1 19.96); valid-action EM 18.10, F1 19.44.
extracted_data.key_findings: Structured knowledge-graph memory plus (1) predicting graph differences, (2) multi-task learning (predicting next graph-diff and valid actions jointly), and (3) an SOS loss that enforces set-permutation invariance together yield substantial improvements on both graph and action prediction; graph-diff simplification is particularly important.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Partial observability and huge action vocab remain challenging; valid-action prediction is harder (larger vocab, more tokens) and metrics are lower than graph prediction. Ablations show model requires all three components; training and decoding are compute-intensive (≈380M params, beam search).

source_info.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

extracted_data.brief_description: A QA-based approach that uses an ALBERT QA model to answer questions about the observation and populate a knowledge graph via post-processing rules; used as a strong baseline for graph construction in JerichoWorld.
extracted_data.citation_title: How to avoid being eaten by a grue: Structured exploration strategies for textual worlds
extracted_data.agent_description: Uses an ALBERT (BERT-family) QA model trained on SQuAD and Jericho-QA to answer template questions about the state; answers are converted to triples via hand-authored rules to build the knowledge graph.
extracted_data.memory_type: Structured knowledge graph (constructed via QA answers + heuristic rules).
extracted_data.memory_integration: Answers from the QA model are post-processed with hand-authored rules to create/update triples in the knowledge-graph memory; memory is essentially extractive from text rather than learned generative updates.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Knowledge-graph prediction overall: graph-level EM 32.79, graph-level F1 35.48; token-level EM 53.58, token-level F1 55.74 (token-level scores comparable to Worldformer; graph-level scores lower).
extracted_data.key_findings: QA-based extractive graph construction yields high token-overlap (good for token metrics) but tends to over-extract and underperform on strict graph-level metrics; extraction fails when information is unobserved/hidden (e.g., contents of closed containers) where generative/predictive memory helps.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Prone to over-extraction (inflates token metrics), brittle when observations omit information (cannot perform look-ahead), depends on hand-authored rules for assembly of graphs.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

extracted_data.brief_description: An adaptation of the Graph-Aided Transformer Agent that encodes graphs and observations with transformer encoders and decodes add/del graph tuples in a Seq2Seq fashion; used as a single-task baseline for graph update prediction.
extracted_data.citation_title: Learning dynamic belief graphs to generalize on text-based games
extracted_data.agent_description: Encoder structure similar to Worldformer (text + graph encoders); single autoregressive decoder trained to predict both additions and deletions as tagged tuples (e.g., <add,...> or <del,...>) in a Seq2Seq manner.
extracted_data.model_name: Transformer-based encoders/decoder (architecture adapted from Graph-Aided Transformer Agent)
extracted_data.memory_type: Structured knowledge graph (belief graph), with explicit add/delete predictions for updates.
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph G_t is input to a graph encoder; a single decoder autoregressively emits add/del triple tokens representing (G_{t+1}-G_t) ∪ (G_t-G_{t+1}). Integration is as standard Seq2Seq conditioning on encoded inputs.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Knowledge-graph prediction overall: graph-level EM 24.06, F1 25.19; token-level EM 35.31, token-level F1 37.10.
extracted_data.key_findings: Using a graph encoder with an autoregressive decoder that predicts add/del operations improves over a plain Seq2Seq baseline (since it predicts fewer tokens on average) but underperforms the Worldformer which uses multi-tasking, graph-diff simplification, and SOS loss.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Single-task Seq2Seq decoding of add/del still predicts more tokens than the graph-diff-only formulation and lacks the multi-task benefits of jointly predicting valid actions.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

extracted_data.brief_description: A single-task sequence-to-sequence baseline that encodes observations and graphs with a bidirectional BERT-based encoder and decodes the next graph or actions with a GPT-2-like autoregressive decoder using standard cross-entropy loss.
extracted_data.agent_description: BERT-like encoder for inputs (observation + graph), GPT-2-like autoregressive decoder to predict next graph or valid actions; trained with standard Seq2Seq cross-entropy (left-to-right) loss.
extracted_data.memory_type: Structured knowledge graph provided as input (treated as part of the sequence input), but no explicit graph-diff or SOS handling.
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph and observation are concatenated/encoded together by the encoder to form a context; decoder autoregressively generates the full target (graph or actions) as a single ordered sequence.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Knowledge-graph prediction overall: graph-level EM 14.29, F1 15.54; token-level EM 18.80, F1 19.96. Valid-action prediction overall: EM 18.10, F1 19.44.
extracted_data.key_findings: Standard Seq2Seq without SOS loss, graph-diff simplification, or multi-tasking performs substantially worse than Worldformer; serves as ablation baseline showing impact of the three Worldformer contributions.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Treats sets (triples/actions) as ordered sequences which is suboptimal (ignores permutation invariance), and must generate longer target sequences leading to poorer accuracy.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

extracted_data.brief_description: A GPT-2 based valid-action generation model fine-tuned on human-authored observation-action transcripts (ClubFloyd dataset) used as a baseline for action prediction transfer.
extracted_data.citation_title: Keep CALM and explore: Language models for action generation in text-based games
extracted_data.agent_description: GPT-2 tuned on a complementary dataset of human play transcripts (ClubFloyd) to generate valid actions given (O_t, A, O_{t+1}) inputs and target V_{t+1}.
extracted_data.memory_integration: No structured memory augmentation reported in this paper; uses direct language-model generation conditioned on recent observations/transitions.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Valid-action prediction (overall): EM 13.79, F1 19.11 (note: CALM trained on a different dataset; shows high variance across games).
extracted_data.key_findings: Language-model-based action generation trained on human transcripts can transfer to some games but shows high variance depending on domain similarity; generally outperformed by Worldformer for valid-action prediction when Worldformer's graph-informed multi-task learning is used.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Not memory-augmented; performance is sensitive to domain similarity between training transcripts and target games and exhibits higher variance across games.

source_info.paper_title: Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models of Textual Environments

extracted_data.brief_description: A rule-based information-extraction baseline that uses OpenIE and hand-authored rules to extract triples from observations to populate knowledge graphs.
extracted_data.citation_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces
extracted_data.agent_description: Non-learned information extraction pipeline that applies OpenIE and handcrafted rules to observations to produce triples for the knowledge graph.
extracted_data.memory_type: Structured knowledge graph (constructed via rule-based extraction from text).
extracted_data.memory_integration: Extraction tools (OpenIE) and manual rules run over the observation text to create/update triples in the knowledge graph; this is a deterministic pipeline rather than a learned memory encoder.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Knowledge-graph prediction overall: graph-level EM 4.70, F1 7.25; token-level EM 13.08, F1 17.50.
extracted_data.key_findings: Rule-based extraction is simple but achieves low graph-level performance; helps with token overlap but performs poorly on strict triple matching and cannot infer unobserved information.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Sensitive to irregular text in games; cannot predict hidden/unrevealed facts; relies on brittle hand-authored rules and OpenIE failures.

extracted_data.brief_description: An on-policy RL agent that builds a dynamic knowledge graph from text observations and uses it both as a state representation (via GAT embeddings) and to constrain template-based natural language action decoding (graph mask). Demonstrated across 28 Jericho games, outperforming a strong template-based baseline on most games.
extracted_data.agent_description: Actor–critic RL agent that encodes observations (GRUs + subword tokens) and a dynamic knowledge graph (embedded with Graph Attention Networks) into a joint state vector s_t; decodes actions by first predicting a template then predicting object tokens conditioned on previous decoded tokens, using a graph-derived mask to limit object choices.
extracted_data.memory_type: dynamic knowledge graph (structured, graph-formed external memory of <subject,relation,object> triples)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph is updated each step from observations (OpenIE + heuristic rules), embedded via Graph Attention Network into vector g_t which is concatenated with encoded observation and score; additionally used to create a graph mask that restricts object choices during template action decoding.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Jericho (suite of human-made Interactive Fiction games, e.g. Zork1)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Across 28 Jericho games KG-A2C matches or outperforms the Template-DQN baseline on 23/28 games; example: Zork1 score = 34 (KG-A2C) vs 9.9 (TDQN). See Table 1 for per-game raw scores.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Template-DQN baseline (no KG): e.g., Zork1 score = 9.9; also ablations without masking/with limited GAT show reduced sample efficiency or lower per-game scores (see ablations).
extracted_data.key_findings: A dynamic knowledge graph used as state and to constrain language generation (graph mask) enables scaling RL agents to very large template-based natural language action spaces; KG-A2C outperforms a strong template-based baseline (TDQN) on most tested games and converges faster when graph attention or graph masking is available. The graph serves dual roles: improved state disambiguation (partial observability, commonsense grounding) and strong pruning of action space during decoding.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Relies on external tools/handicaps: Jericho's valid-action detection and load/save 'handicap' were used in experiments; knowledge extraction depends on OpenIE plus heuristics which may be imperfect for IF narrative text; random addition of unseen objects to the mask (probability p_m) is needed to avoid over-pruning; supervised valid-action auxiliary losses remain necessary—memory alone did not remove the need for that supervision.

source_info.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

extracted_data.brief_description: Prior approach that builds a knowledge graph during exploration and uses it as a state representation for deep Q-learning in text-adventure games, showing that graph-based state representations can help with partial observability and commonsense reasoning.
extracted_data.citation_title: Playing text-adventure games with graph-based deep reinforcement learning
extracted_data.agent_description: A deep Q-learning agent that constructs a knowledge graph from observations and uses the graph as the agent's state representation for value estimation; introduced in prior work by the same authors and cited as motivating the current KG-A2C design.
extracted_data.memory_type: knowledge graph (structured external memory of extracted triples)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph built from observations and used as the agent's state representation for Q-value estimation (no template-based decoding integration in that work as described here).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Previously evaluated on text-adventure game(s) in earlier work (single domain experiments cited); in this paper it is cited but not re-evaluated on the Jericho suite.
extracted_data.key_findings: Prior work established that knowledge-graph state representations assist with partial observability and commonsense reasoning in text games; motivated the use of graphs here but did not address large template-based generation.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Earlier KG-DQN was evaluated in a single domain and did not incorporate the graph to constrain natural language generation (template decoding), limiting applicability to combinatorially large language action spaces.

source_info.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

extracted_data.name_full: Advantage Actor Critic without Knowledge Graph (ablation)
extracted_data.brief_description: An ablation variant that removes the knowledge graph entirely so the state embedding is just the encoded observation + score and no graph mask is used for decoding actions.
extracted_data.agent_description: Standard A2C agent variant in the paper's ablations: observation encoder and critic remain, but no knowledge-graph construction, no GAT embedding, and no graph mask during action decoding.
extracted_data.memory_type: none
extracted_data.memory_integration: none (no external or structured memory)
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Zork1 (ablation experiments); evaluated as part of the ablation suite
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: On Zork1 the ablated A2C (no KG) achieved approximately the same asymptotic score (~34 points) as KG-A2C in the reported runs but converged slightly slower—i.e., knowledge-graph components improved learning speed though asymptotic performance on Zork1 was similar.
extracted_data.key_findings: Removing the knowledge graph did not necessarily reduce final asymptotic score on Zork1 in these runs, but agents with access to either graph attention or the graph mask converged faster (better sample efficiency).
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Although asymptotic performance on Zork1 was similar in this experiment, the knowledge graph provided benefits in convergence speed and broader generalization across many games; results may be game-dependent and some games show larger gaps.

source_info.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

extracted_data.brief_description: Ablation that builds the knowledge graph and uses GAT embeddings in the state but does not apply the graph-derived mask to constrain object decoding during template-based action generation.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same as KG-A2C but the graph mask (which restricts object predictions to entities present in the graph mask) is ablated; the knowledge graph still contributes to the state via the GAT embedding.
extracted_data.memory_type: dynamic knowledge graph (used for state only, mask removed)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph is built and embedded into state (GAT) but is not used to constrain object decoding (no mask).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Zork1 and the full Jericho suite (ablation reported on Zork1 and a larger study across games)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: On Zork1 (Table 2) KG-A2C-no-mask scored 27 (vs KG-A2C-full 34 on Zork1); in the larger study KG-A2C-full outperformed no-mask on 10 games and was outperformed on 6.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: KG-A2C-full (with mask + GAT) Zork1 score = 34 (examples); TDQN baseline Zork1 = 9.9
extracted_data.key_findings: Removing the graph mask reduces performance on some games (e.g., Zork1: 27 vs 34) and indicates that using the graph to constrain action generation is a key mechanism for effective exploration in large template action spaces.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Without the mask the decoder must consider many more candidate objects, hurting sample efficiency and sometimes final performance; however, mask usefulness is game-dependent (no-mask outperformed full on some games).

source_info.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

extracted_data.brief_description: Ablation that constructs the knowledge graph and uses the graph mask but removes the Graph Attention Network (GAT) embedding; the graph is still used to constrain decoding but not embedded via attention.
extracted_data.agent_description: Knowledge graph is constructed and the graph mask is applied during decoding, but GAT-based embedding of the graph is removed; the agent uses the same state embedding as the A2C ablation (no GAT component).
extracted_data.memory_type: dynamic knowledge graph (used for masking but not GAT-based embedding)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph is built and used for action masking; GAT embedding component is removed (graph not embedded via self-attention into g_t).
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Zork1 (ablation studied) and general Jericho evaluation (component ablations discussed)
extracted_data.key_findings: Retaining either graph attention or graph masking helps convergence; removing GAT while keeping mask still provides benefits over having no graph at all (improves sample efficiency), but the paper does not report a single aggregated numeric score for the no-gat variant in the main table.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Specific numeric comparisons for the no-gat variant are not provided in the paper's main result tables; effect seen primarily as modest changes in learning speed/sample efficiency.

source_info.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

extracted_data.brief_description: Variant that retains the knowledge graph and masking but disables the supervised auxiliary valid-action losses for template and object prediction, forcing the agent to explore the template space without valid-action supervision.
extracted_data.agent_description: KG-A2C variant where the valid-action cross-entropy losses L_T and L_O are disabled and entropy is calculated over the full action set, so the agent must learn without access to Jericho's valid-action supervision.
extracted_data.memory_type: dynamic knowledge graph (retained)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph used for state and mask as in KG-A2C, but no supervised valid-action training signal provided.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Fails to achieve any score when trained for the same number of steps as other agents (reported as 'fails to achieve any score').
extracted_data.key_findings: Access to supervised valid-action signals (the auxiliary cross-entropy losses using valid actions from Jericho) is important; a knowledge graph by itself was insufficient to enable exploration of the huge template action space without that auxiliary supervision.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Indicates that memory (knowledge graph) alone does not solve exploration/pruning; external supervision (valid-action detection) or comparable signal is required to learn effectively in the large template action space.

source_info.paper_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces

extracted_data.brief_description: Variant that discards the template action space and decodes actions word-by-word up to four words, with teacher-forcing and a supervised valid-action loss, to test whether template structure is necessary.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same KG-A2C architecture but action decoder generates sequences token-by-token from the full vocabulary (max 4 tokens), uses teacher-forcing with probability p_valid and a supervised valid-action loss computed by sampling a valid action as a training target.
extracted_data.memory_type: dynamic knowledge graph (retained)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Graph used for state embedding and mask (where applicable), but action space is full-token sequence rather than template+object filling.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Reached low asymptotic performance (~8 points on Zork1), significantly worse than template-based agents despite teacher forcing and longer training.
extracted_data.key_findings: Using the full token-level action space (sequence decoding) performs poorly even with KG and teacher-forcing; the template action space is necessary for effective exploration in extremely large natural language action spaces.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Sequence decoding expands the action space by orders of magnitude compared to template-based actions, making exploration infeasible in practice even with memory and supervised signals.

extracted_data.brief_description: A four-agent system that decomposes game-solving into summarization, area selection, action selection, and action validation; agents exchange structured representations (summaries and selected-area encodings) via prompts to limit memory growth and produce legal actions for a novel text-described board game (Goedendag).
extracted_data.agent_description: Four task-specialized language-model agents: M1 summarization (extracts a formalized game representation rep(G)), M2 area selection (selects a fixed-size subset/area and returns rep(G')), M3 action selection (chooses an action from rep(G')), and M4 action validation (checks rule compliance). Agents collaborate through a tree-of-thoughts planning procedure, passing representations in prompt context.
extracted_data.memory_type: task-specific local state memory (summarized representation and fixed-size selected-area state subset)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Memory is held and communicated as structured text representations included in the prompt context passed between agents (context concatenation). Area selection constrains retained state to a fixed-size subset to enforce linear memory growth instead of remembering the exponential full-state tree.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Goedendag (novel Stratego-like board game described in text)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Win rate: ~65% overall against benchmark algorithms; pairwise win rates vs benchmarks reported in Table 1: Ours vs Direct query 0.647 ± 0.225, Ours vs CoT query 0.611 ± 0.201, Ours vs Random 0.662 ± 0.136. Error rate: 0.0575 (average across 200 games). Tokens per game (cost): 26211. Fine-tuning of agents produced an additional ~10% win-rate improvement.
extracted_data.key_findings: Decomposing into task-specific agents and keeping only task-relevant representations (summaries and a fixed-size selected area) effectively mitigates long-term memorization burdens: the approach attains ~65% win rate vs several baselines while keeping error rate low. Independent fine-tuning of task agents yields larger gains than an end-to-end model (≈+10% after fine-tuning with ~1000 samples).
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: High inference cost (large number of LM tokens per game) due to multi-agent back-and-forth; no explicit external memory store was implemented (only constrained in-prompt representations); experiments limited to a single newly designed game (Goedendag) and resource-limited fine-tuning (only area and action agents were fine-tuned). The paper notes constructing an explicit memory bank as future work.

source_info.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

extracted_data.name_full: Selected-Area State Memory via Area Selection Agent (M2)
extracted_data.brief_description: A memory strategy where the agent selects a fixed-size area (subset of game states) around a center tile and retains only that area representation (rep(G')) as the operative memory for decision tasks, preventing exponential growth of retained states.
extracted_data.agent_description: Language-model agent that, given the summarized representation rep(G), selects a center tile and returns a structured representation rep(G') encoding the local sub-area (states, visible pieces, allowable actions) which restricts subsequent reasoning and action choices to that subset.
extracted_data.memory_type: localized, bounded-state memory (fixed-size selected area / substate)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Selected-area representation rep(G') is concatenated into prompts for the action-selection and validation agents; by fixing area size, memory retained grows linearly with decisions rather than exponentially with full-tree expansion.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: When included in the pipeline (i.e., with area selection), overall method error rate = 0.0575 and win rates as reported for 'Ours' (see main entry).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Omission (Ours w/o area) yields error rate = 0.313 (Table 2) and a measurable decrease in win-rate (paper reports roughly a ~2% decrease in win rate when area selection is removed in ablation). Cost (tokens/game) reduced when removing area selection (23519 vs 26211) but at severe error-rate penalty.
extracted_data.key_findings: Area selection dramatically reduces rule-violation errors by restricting the action-selection space to a manageable local subset (observed ~5x increase in error rate when area selection removed). It therefore acts as an implicit memory-reduction mechanism that improves reliability of LM-chosen actions.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Area selection trades off inference cost and completeness — it requires multiple agent interactions and does not preserve long-term global history beyond repeated summarization; it is not an explicit external memory bank and may miss relevant distant-state information.

source_info.paper_title: Reasoning, Memorization, and Fine-Tuning Language Models for Non-Cooperative Games

extracted_data.name_full: Structured Game Summarization Representation (rep(G)) from Summarization Agent (M1)
extracted_data.brief_description: A standardized, structured textual representation of the current game state (rep(G)) produced by a summarization agent that encodes pieces, positions, allowable actions, and objectives to reduce cognitive load on downstream agents.
extracted_data.agent_description: Language-model agent tasked with converting the raw textual game-state description into a consistent, formalized game representation rep(G) that is then used by area-selection, action-selection, and validation agents.
extracted_data.memory_type: structured summary retained in prompt context (short-term, formalized episodic-like representation)
extracted_data.memory_integration: rep(G) is concatenated into downstream task prompts (M2, M3, M4) to provide a consistent state encoding across agents; this centralized summary reduces the amount of raw state text that each agent must parse.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Including summarization improves win rate: ablation removing summarization ('Ours w/o summary') reduces win rate by ≈5% relative to the full method; with summarization the method achieves the reported ~65% win rate. Error rates: interestingly, 'Ours w/o summary' shows error rate 0.0417 in Table 2 (slightly lower than full method's 0.0575) but overall win-rate suffers, indicating the summary primarily helps strategic decision quality.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Ours w/o summary: error rate = 0.0417, win-rate decreases by ≈5% (Table 1 rows for ablation), tokens per game: 17881 (lower cost).
extracted_data.key_findings: A dedicated summarization agent producing a consistent rep(G) helps downstream decision agents reason more effectively and increases win probability, even if raw rule-violation error behavior may vary; the task-specific summarized representation reduces per-agent cognitive load and contributes to overall performance.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Summarization adds extra LM calls and token cost; the paper observes trade-offs between cost, error-rate, and strategic performance and notes that removing the summarization task reduces token costs but hurts win rate.

extracted_data.brief_description: An actor-critic RL agent that augments its observation embedding with an explicit knowledge graph of observed world facts, embedding the graph via graph-attention and combining it with a recurrent observation encoder to produce action logits and a value estimate.
extracted_data.citation_title: Graph constrained reinforcement learning for natural language action spaces
extracted_data.agent_description: Advantage-Actor Critic architecture augmented with an external knowledge graph that is updated from observations (rooms, objects, inventory, relations) and embedded via a graph-attention network; observations are also embedded via a GRU and concatenated with the KG embedding to inform actor and critic heads.
extracted_data.memory_type: structured external knowledge graph (observed-world facts)
extracted_data.memory_integration: The agent updates a knowledge graph from textual observations; the KG is embedded with a graph-attention mechanism and combined with a recurrent GRU embedding of concatenated observation components to produce policy and value outputs.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Thief-Only: thief score 93.2% (std 9.3), adv 3.8%, avg game steps 24.7; Adv-Only: thief 3.8%, adv 99.3% (std 4.8), avg steps 33.4; Both trained (single KG-A2C trained on both rewards): thief 88.2% (std 10.3), adv 68.6% (std 7.8), avg steps 33.1.
extracted_data.key_findings: KG-A2C provides an effective external memory for text-roleplaying tasks: when trained on a single character it achieves high character-specific scores; however, a single KG-A2C trained naively on multiple (conflicting) reward functions degrades performance (both-trained case).
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: When trained on multiple character reward signals without disentangling (no prompts), the single KG-A2C tends to attempt to maximize all rewards and fails to differentiate characters; the paper does not report KG-A2C variants with different memory modalities beyond KG.

source_info.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: A single actor model built on the KG-A2C family that learns per-character soft prompts (learned embedding vectors) and produces a stack of character-specific action logits and value estimates so it can emulate multiple characters and be directed by a prompt.
extracted_data.agent_description: Extends KG-A2C by (1) learning soft character prompts (trainable embedding vectors) concatenated with observation embeddings to produce character-conditioned internal states, and (2) producing a stack of action logits and value predictions (one per character) so the single model can output per-character policies; trained by rotating character-specific reward functions and updating only the selected logits/value for each episode.
extracted_data.memory_type: 1) external knowledge graph (inherited from KG-A2C); 2) soft character prompts (learned embedding vectors stored as parameters)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Knowledge graph integration as in KG-A2C; soft character prompts are stored embedding vectors p_i concatenated with the current observation embedding o (cat(o, p_i)) and passed through trainable weights to produce a character-specific internal state; actor and critic heads produce per-character logits/values.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Thespian agent (single model trained on both characters) when prompted: Thief Prompt: thief score 92.9% (std 17.4), adv 3.8%, avg steps 22.1; Adventurer Prompt: thief 4.3%, adv 99.1% (std 11.1), avg steps 32.2; Random Prompt: thief 10.7% (std 12.8), adv 72.7% (std 37.5), avg steps 26.5.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Base KG-A2C trained on both characters (no prompting): thief 88.2% (std 10.3), adv 68.6% (std 7.8), avg steps 33.1.
extracted_data.key_findings: The thespian agent, using soft prompts plus KG memory, matches the performance of single-character KG-A2C models while being a single model that can switch characters via prompts; it avoids the degradation observed when a KG-A2C is trained on both reward functions without disentangling.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Performance depends on correct prompt input (random prompts lead to poor behavior); requires learning and storing per-character prompts; variance can be higher (noted std.dev for thief prompt). The approach relies on the underlying KG-A2C memory; no alternate memory modalities were compared in experiments.

source_info.paper_title: Thespian: Multi-Character Text Role-Playing Game Agents

extracted_data.brief_description: A lightweight attention module that, given a frozen pre-trained thespian agent, learns to blend the frozen per-character raw action logits (and uses observation-conditioned attention) to produce a new character policy in few-shot fashion without altering core model weights.
extracted_data.agent_description: With the core thespian agent frozen, the module projects stacked observation embeddings and stacked per-character action logits into a joint space, computes observation-to-character attention scores, and forms a weighted average of raw logits (separately for verb and object) to produce final action distributions; also learns a new soft prompt for the new character.
extracted_data.memory_type: frozen stored per-character logits and soft prompts (parameterized memory of previously learned policies)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Operates on frozen outputs of the thespian (stacked per-character logits and trained prompt embeddings); projects observations and logits through FFNs, computes attention S = softmax((h_O * h_A) / m), and combines S^T * A (with observation scaling alpha_obs) to get final logits; does not alter frozen core weights.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: LIGHT (Thief/Adventurer -> new Rogue character; three map variants)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Few-shot learning of new 'Rogue' character: thespian attention converged by ~1,500 steps in adventurer-first and alternating maps; overall few-shot training limited to 3,000 steps; claimed to be ~10x faster than training the unfrozen agent (which converges after ~15,000 steps).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Unfrozen thespian agent trained from same starting point (no attention) required ~15,000 steps to converge to similar performance and suffered catastrophic forgetting of pre-trained characters; an approach rotating games for all three characters took >40,000 steps to converge.
extracted_data.key_findings: Using frozen pre-trained character policies as a form of memory and learning an observation-conditioned blending via attention yields fast (few-shot) learning of a new character (~10x faster) while preserving previous character behaviors; operating on raw logits (not softmaxed probabilities) is important for successful blending.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Performance can be biased by pre-training regimen (overfitting to the last-trained character can skew attention weights); requires careful hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, value-loss coefficient); some alternative attention formulations (over prompts or over probabilities) failed or were inferior.

extracted_data.name_full: Large Language Models augmented with memory, belief, and tools
extracted_data.brief_description: A general class of methods in which LLMs are augmented with auxiliary memory or belief modules and external tools to support multi-step reasoning, multi-agent interaction, and embodied tasks; mentioned in Related Work as having excelled in some open-domain and simple imperfect-information games.
extracted_data.agent_description: Generic description (not a single implemented agent in this paper): LLMs augmented with extra modules (memory, belief trackers, or tool interfaces) to store/retrieve past events or inferred states and to call external functionality for improved decision-making.
extracted_data.memory_type: described generically as memory and belief (no specific taxonomy given in this paper)
extracted_data.memory_integration: described at a high level only (memory/belief/tools augment LLMs; specific integration details not provided in this paper)
extracted_data.key_findings: The paper cites prior work claiming that LLMs augmented with memory, belief, and tools have excelled in multi-step reasoning and in some open-domain survival games and simple two-player imperfect information games, motivating the present study's use of belief (ToM) and an external tool.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: No implementation details or quantitative comparisons are given in this paper for this generic class; the mention is in related work only.

source_info.paper_title: Evaluating and Enhancing LLMs Agent Based on Theory of Mind in Guandan: A Multi-Player Cooperative Game Under Imperfect Information

extracted_data.name_full: LLM-based embodied agent with Theory-of-Mind planning (this paper)
extracted_data.brief_description: A modular LLM agent that uses a State Interpreter, a ToM Planner (first- and second-order belief inference from game history), and a Plan Evaluator; it ingests game rules, converted human-readable observations and game history, and optionally invokes an external RL-based action recommender tool to narrow valid actions.
extracted_data.agent_description: Modular language-model-based embodied agent for the Guandan card game: components include Current State Interpreter (converts low-level state/history to readable text), ToM Planner (infers beliefs of other agents from history at zero/first/second order), Planning/Evaluator (scores candidate plans/actions), and an external action-recommender RL tool to rank valid actions; used in zero-shot (no fine-tuning) experiments with multiple LLMs.
extracted_data.memory_type: short-term contextual memory: explicit game history (Obs_r and His_r) presented as readable text; additionally inferred belief state (ToM) is treated as an internal belief-memory representation
extracted_data.memory_integration: History and converted observations are concatenated into the LLM prompt/context (context-window based); beliefs inferred by the ToM Planner are appended to prompt and provided as additional context to the Planner/Evaluator; no separate persistent external memory store is reported.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Guandan (Chinese multi-player cooperative imperfect-information card game)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Example: GPT-4-turbo with 2nd-order ToM (beliefs from history in context) achieved average score +2.17 vs the three baselines (Table 1). GPT-3.5-turbo 1st-ToM avg +0.98, 2nd-ToM avg +0.97 (Table 1). Using ToM improved results over vanilla planning across almost all tested models.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Vanilla (zero-order planning without ToM belief input) example: GPT-4-turbo (Vanilla) avg +1.26; GPT-3.5-turbo (Vanilla) avg +0.68 (Table 1). (These are the same experimental setups but without the ToM-inferred belief context.)
extracted_data.key_findings: Providing LLM agents with explicit belief information derived from game history (first- and second-order ToM planning) consistently improves performance over vanilla planning across nearly all tested models; incorporating an external RL-based action recommender tool to limit candidate actions also substantially improves agent performance by mitigating LLMs' difficulty with very long valid-action lists.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: No persistent/long-term memory module is implemented; the 'memory' is limited to game history provided in the prompt (context window) and inferred belief states. LLMs still trail state-of-the-art RL (Danzero+) except for GPT-4 which approaches but does not surpass Danzero+ (GPT-4 2nd-ToM scored -0.88 vs Danzero+ in Table 1). LLMs struggle when the valid-action list is very long (often focus on early items), motivating the external action-recommender tool.

extracted_data.name_full: LLM agent (language agent proposed in 'Large Language Models Are Neurosymbolic Reasoners')
extracted_data.brief_description: A prompt-driven language-agent that uses a large language model (GPT-3.5-turbo) in a zero-shot way to play text-based games by selecting actions from a provided valid-action set and invoking external symbolic modules (calculator, navigator, sorter, knowledge-base).
extracted_data.agent_description: A role-initialized, prompt-driven agent that at each timestep receives the observation, inventory state, score, and a constrained valid-action set and must choose a single action; it can call external symbolic modules (Calculation, Navigation, Sorting, Knowledge Base) as actions. Implemented by prompting GPT-3.5-turbo (zero-shot, no extra training).
extracted_data.memory_type: limited short-term / internal context (no explicit external memory augmentation)
extracted_data.memory_integration: State and inventory are provided each timestep via prompt concatenation (observation + inventory + valid action set + role initialization). No persistent external memory or retrieval mechanism was implemented; memory relies on the LLM's context window and the prompt-provided inventory/observation.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: TextWorldExpress text-based games (Arithmetic, MapReader, Sorting, Text World Common Sense - TWC)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: average score: 0.88 (normalized) on Test set; average steps: 7 (GPT-3.5-turbo, constrained prompts, Table 5 / Table 7). Note: reported performance is for the prompt-driven agent using symbolic modules, not for any explicit memory augmentation.
extracted_data.key_findings: The prompt-driven LLM agent achieves strong average performance (≈0.88 normalized) using symbolic modules in a zero-shot manner without additional training. The paper attributes some failure modes (especially in MapReader and Sorting) to the agent's limited memory capacity (forgetting routes from the navigation module and losing sorted order), and shows that constrained prompts improve performance and reduce steps.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: No explicit memory augmentation implemented; limited memory capacity in the agent causes forgetting of previously obtained symbolic-module outputs (e.g., route information) and difficulty retaining full sorted orders across steps, leading to extra queries and inefficient behavior. The paper notes need for more sophisticated/long-term memory to address these issues.

extracted_data.brief_description: Language-model-driven interactive agents augmented with a persistent natural-language memory stream (observations), periodic higher-level reflections, and stored plans; retrieval and synthesis of memories condition the LLM to produce coherent, long-horizon, socially emergent behavior in a Sims-like sandbox.
extracted_data.agent_description: An agent architecture built around a large language model (gpt-3.5-turbo) that records all perceptions as natural-language memory objects (observations), assigns importance scores at creation, periodically synthesizes higher-level reflections from accumulated events, generates hierarchical plans, and retrieves a compact subset of memories (including observations, reflections, and plans) to condition moment-to-moment action, dialogue, and re-planning.
extracted_data.memory_type: Long-term episodic/diary-style memory stream stored as natural-language entries (observations), plus derived hierarchical reflections and stored temporal plans (i.e., episodic + semantic summaries + procedural plans).
extracted_data.memory_integration: External memory stream with embedding- and LM-based indexing: each memory stores text, creation timestamp, last-access timestamp, and an importance score; retrieval ranks memories by a weighted combination of recency (exponential decay), importance (LM-produced integer), and relevance (embedding cosine similarity to a query), then concatenates top-ranked memories into the prompt (context-window conditioning). Reflections and plans are written back into the same memory stream.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Smallville sandbox (Sims-like) with an interview-based believability evaluation (no standard text-game benchmark)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: TrueSkill (believability) μ = 29.89, σ = 0.72 for the full architecture (observations + reflections + planning); information-diffusion: Isabella party known by 52% of agents at end of 2-day sim; Sam candidacy known by 32%.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Ablations (same evaluation): no reflections: μ = 26.88 (σ = 0.69); no reflection & no planning: μ = 25.64 (σ = 0.68); fully ablated baseline (no observation, no reflection, no planning): μ = 21.21 (σ = 0.70); crowdworker-authored baseline: μ = 22.95 (σ = 0.69).
extracted_data.key_findings: Storing and retrieving a natural-language memory stream plus generating periodic higher-level reflections and explicit planning substantially improves believability and emergent social behavior compared to ablations without these components; reflection and planning both contribute critically (ablation performance degrades monotonically as components are removed). Retrieval failures and occasional memory embellishments are dominant error modes.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Not evaluated on standard text-adventure benchmarks; expensive in tokens and computation for multi-agent multi-day sims; retrieval failures (omitting relevant memories), rare hallucinated embellishments, context-window limits (necessitating retrieval instead of full dump), potential brittleness to memory/ prompt hacking, and some misapplied social/physical norms due to underspecified environment states.

extracted_data.name_full: Large language models applied to interactive fiction and text-adventure games (mentioned)
extracted_data.brief_description: The paper notes prior uses of large language models to create interactive fiction and text-adventure games, but treats these as first-order prompting applications that lack dynamic long-term memory and thus are a poor fit for long-horizon coherence.
extracted_data.agent_description: Referenced class of systems where pretrained LMs are used to generate interactive narratives or act in text-adventure environments (cited in related work), typically via prompt templates/few-shot or chain-of-thought prompting without a persistent, dynamically updated memory stream.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: generic text-adventure / interactive fiction (no specific benchmark evaluation reported in this paper)
extracted_data.key_findings: The paper observes that prior efforts using LLMs for interactive fiction/text-adventure tasks have been effective for generating momentary behavior but do not address long-term conditioning on extensive past experience due to context-window limits; motivating the need for external memory/retrieval and reflection.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: First-order prompting approaches struggle with long-term coherence because the full history does not fit in the model context window; no memory augmentation or performance comparisons provided in this paper for text-game settings.

extracted_data.name_full: Retrieval-augmented and summarization-based augmentations of LMs (mentioned: Demonstrate-Search-Predict, recursive summarization)
extracted_data.brief_description: The paper cites prior work that augments language models with static knowledge bases plus retrieval (Demonstrate-Search-Predict) or with hierarchical summarization (recursively summarizing books) as related approaches to extend effective context beyond the model's window.
extracted_data.agent_description: Prior methods that couple retrieval modules or recursive summarization with language models to supply additional context or compressed long-form content; referenced as antecedents to the generative agents' dynamic memory + retrieval + reflection pipeline.
extracted_data.memory_type: static knowledge base (retrieval-augmented) and summarization-derived compressed memory
extracted_data.memory_integration: External retrieval of documents/knowledge and recursive summarization to compress long texts before feeding into the LM; contrasted with the paper's dynamic episodic memory stream and embedding-based retrieval.
extracted_data.key_findings: These approaches show ways to augment LMs with extra context (retrieval and summarization), but they are typically static or summarization-centric rather than supporting continuously updated, per-agent episodic memory plus periodic reflective synthesis as required for believable long-term agent behavior.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Static KB retrieval and summarization do not by themselves address the need for dynamically updated, importance-weighted episodic memories and higher-level reflections for continual agent coherence; the paper positions its memory + reflection + planning architecture as extending these prior ideas.

extracted_data.name_full: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) KG-A2C Agent with a Hot Expert
extracted_data.brief_description: A Mixture-of-Experts agent that ensembles multiple frozen, role-aligned KG-A2C experts plus one trainable 'hot' expert; an attention module (with value seeding) mixes experts' action distributions to enable rapid few-shot transfer to new role-playing tasks in open-ended text worlds.
extracted_data.agent_description: An MoE built on KG-A2C experts: multiple frozen, pre-trained role-aligned KG-A2C policies produce action logits and critic value estimates; an attention module projects observations and expert logits, multiplies expert action distributions by each expert's critic-predicted expected return (value seeding), computes attention weights, linearly mixes the scaled distributions and the trainable 'hot' expert's logits, and samples actions. The attention modules and the hot expert are trained (few-shot) on the new role while the frozen experts remain fixed.
extracted_data.memory_type: knowledge graph (external persistent memory constructed from observations)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Each KG-A2C expert maintains/updates a world knowledge graph from observations; the KG is embedded via a graph-attention mechanism and combined with a GRU-based observation encoder to form the expert's state representation that feeds actor and critic; these KG-augmented expert outputs are used by the MoE attention module.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: LIGHT-based custom open-ended text role-playing environment (built on the LIGHT environment)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: MoE achieves higher zero-shot test-time score and greater few-shot sample efficiency than baselines (New KG-A2C trained from scratch and fine-tuned individual KG-A2C experts); best MoE runs reach maximum scores in most seeds (e.g., reported average best-agent scores 47.0 in Appendix Table for target roles) and discover deeper rewards that New KG-A2C fails to reach within the same training budget.
extracted_data.key_findings: Combining multiple frozen KG-augmented experts via an attention module plus a trainable 'hot' expert yields superior zero-shot and few-shot adaptation to new role tasks compared to training a KG-A2C from scratch or fine-tuning single experts; value seeding (multiplying expert action distributions by their critic values) helps leverage expert knowledge; MoE is robust to many irrelevant (distractor) experts but fails when no relevant experts exist.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Premature convergence and expert starvation (attention overweighting an expert whose early reward is high) were observed and required interventions (epsilon-greedy sampling and an added cross-entropy loss term to flatten logits). The MoE can be degraded by adversarially removing relevant experts (i.e., when no expert contains useful knowledge for the target role it effectively reduces to training from scratch and experts can hamper exploration). Additional training time is incurred because multiple expert forward passes are computed each step.

source_info.paper_title: A Mixture-of-Experts Approach to Few-Shot Task Transfer in Open-Ended Text Worlds

extracted_data.brief_description: An actor-critic RL agent for text games that constructs a persistent knowledge graph of observed world facts and embeds it (via graph attention) to augment observation encoding for action selection and value prediction.
extracted_data.agent_description: A reinforcement learning agent that encodes textual observations with a GRU and simultaneously updates a persistent world knowledge graph (triples of observed facts); the KG is embedded using a graph-attention mechanism and combined with the GRU embedding to produce state representations for the actor and critic heads.
extracted_data.memory_type: knowledge graph (external persistent memory of world state)
extracted_data.memory_integration: The agent updates a knowledge graph from observations at each timestep; the graph is embedded via graph attention and concatenated/combined with recurrent observation embeddings to form the agent's state input to actor and critic.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Used as the base agent across experiments on the LIGHT-based open-ended text role-playing environment and referenced for text-adventure game benchmarks (e.g., Jericho family in related work).
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: KG-A2C (when trained from scratch on a new role) discovers some early rewards but is less sample-efficient and often fails to find deeper role-specific rewards within the same training budget compared to the MoE agent that leverages multiple frozen KG-A2C experts.
extracted_data.key_findings: KG-A2C's KG memory provides effective state representation for role-aligned behaviors and serves as the memory substrate enabling the MoE approach; however, a single KG-A2C trained from scratch is less sample-efficient and attains lower overall reward discovery in few-shot transfer compared to the MoE ensemble of KG-A2C experts.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Single KG-A2C agents can struggle to discover rewards deep in the environment within limited training steps; subject to typical RL exploration challenges (sparse rewards) that are partially mitigated via story-shaping during pretraining.

extracted_data.name_full: diff history (Unix diff-based text delta memory)
extracted_data.brief_description: A task-agnostic memory representation that replaces most full-text observations in an LM interaction history with Unix diff-produced line-by-line textual deltas, then supplies these deltas in-context as the agent's memory for action generation.
extracted_data.agent_description: A decoder-only LM policy (GPT-2 variants in the paper) finetuned to predict natural language actions conditioned on an instruction and an interaction history where, after an initial anchor full-text observation, subsequent observations are represented as Unix diff deltas (textual changes) instead of raw full-text observations.
extracted_data.model_name: GPT-2 (127M) (also experiments with reinitialized weights / from-scratch and larger training data sizes)
extracted_data.memory_type: compressed contextual memory: relational textual deltas (Unix diff)
extracted_data.memory_integration: concatenated into the model context window as tokens (context-window memory augmentation replacing full-text observations with diff tokens, using special <|action|> and <|observation|> markers)
extracted_data.benchmark_name: NetHack (via NetHack Language Wrapper / LangHack dataset) and BabyAI-Text
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: NetHack (pretrained, 32M expert actions): mean score 1885 ± 213 (best history horizon 15); NetHack (from-scratch, 32M): mean score 1203 ± 214; NetHack (pretrained, 64M expert actions / LangHack-2x): mean score 2766 ± 481; BabyAI-Text: reaches peak performance with ≥25% fewer FLOPs than full-text baseline (in 1k and 5k demonstration settings).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Full-text history baseline (pretrained, 32M): mean score 31 ± 5 (best history horizon 8) on NetHack; full-text + auxiliary world-model (pretrained): mean score 344 ± 52 (best horizon 8).
extracted_data.key_findings: Representing history as Unix-style diffs yields major benefits: (1) in NetHack, diff-history LM agents achieve state-of-the-art neural-agent performance while being finetuned on orders-of-magnitude fewer labeled actions (e.g., matching/approaching SOTA with 1,800× fewer labels reported elsewhere), (2) diff acts as token-level compression in high-dimensional text (NetHack: ~460% reduction in tokens per observation), extending effective memory horizon at fixed context length, (3) in BabyAI-Text diff agents require ≥25% fewer optimizer steps / FLOPs to reach equivalent performance in low-data settings, and (4) ablating diff observations drastically reduces performance (a reported ~98% drop in NetHack mean score for the full-text ablation).
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Diff history benefits rely on structured, line-oriented textual observations; in low-dimensional environments (BabyAI-Text) diff increased average tokens per observation (about 210% increase) though still improved sample-efficiency; method assumes observations can be expressed in text and may not help for narrative/unstructured text unless preprocessed into structured form; combining diff-history with auxiliary world-model objective can sometimes reduce diff-agent performance (diff + world-model decreased mean NetHack score versus diff alone in some settings).

source_info.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

extracted_data.name_full: full-text interaction history (raw observations as memory)
extracted_data.brief_description: Baseline memory representation in which all past environment text observations are provided in full (raw) form in the LM context window as the agent's memory.
extracted_data.agent_description: A decoder-only LM (GPT-2 in experiments) finetuned to predict actions conditioned on instructions and interaction histories composed of raw full-text observations and actions (no diff compression).
extracted_data.memory_type: raw contextual memory: full-text observations
extracted_data.memory_integration: concatenated into the model context window as tokens (context-window memory consisting of raw observations and previous actions)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: NetHack (pretrained, 32M expert actions): mean score 31 ± 5 (best history horizon 8) when trained only on action prediction; in NetHack adding an auxiliary world-model objective improved this to mean score 344 ± 52.
extracted_data.key_findings: Full-text history agents perform poorly in high-dimensional NetHack when trained only on action prediction (very low mean score), but an auxiliary causal world-model objective (predicting observations) substantially improves the full-text agent (from mean ≈31 to ≈344), narrowing but not closing the gap with diff-history agents; full-text contexts are token-heavy (NetHack mean tokens per observation ~573) limiting effective history length under fixed context sizes.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Full-text observations are verbose and information-sparse in high-dimensional environments, causing context-length limitations and poor sample efficiency; even with auxiliary objectives, full-text agents underperform diff-history agents in NetHack; large token counts per observation force truncation or limit memory horizon.

source_info.paper_title: diff History for Neural Language Agents

extracted_data.name_full: diff history with auxiliary causal world-model objective
extracted_data.brief_description: A variant that augments diff-history finetuning with an additional causal world-model prediction objective (predicting diff observations), to probe whether auxiliary modeling of observations improves representation learning.
extracted_data.agent_description: Same LM architecture (GPT-2) finetuned jointly on action prediction and an auxiliary causal world-model loss where the model also predicts subsequent diff observation tokens; diff representations remain the memory format.
extracted_data.memory_type: compressed contextual memory: relational textual deltas (Unix diff), plus auxiliary predictive objective
extracted_data.memory_integration: diff tokens concatenated into context window; training includes additional loss terms to predict diff observation tokens (teacher-forcing world-model loss)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: NetHack (pretrained, 32M): mean score 1094 ± 160 (best history horizon 15) when trained with both action prediction and the auxiliary world-model objective.
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Diff-only (pretrained, 32M): mean score 1885 ± 213 (action prediction only) — so adding the world-model objective reduced mean score for the diff agent in the reported experiments.
extracted_data.key_findings: Adding the auxiliary causal world-model objective greatly helps full-text agents (improving their NetHack mean score ~10×), but for diff-history agents the auxiliary objective reduced performance compared to action-prediction-only finetuning, suggesting diff-history alone provides a stronger signal for action modeling in NetHack; overall, auxiliary objectives can change representation learning dynamics and may narrow gaps between memory formats depending on environment and data.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: The auxiliary world-model objective does not universally improve diff-history agent performance and can even degrade it; choice of training objectives interacts nontrivially with memory representation and environment complexity.

extracted_data.brief_description: An RL agent that maintains a dynamically updated knowledge graph (KG) representing objects and relations in the game world and uses that structured memory to constrain/guide action selection within an Advantage Actor-Critic (A2C) framework.
extracted_data.citation_title: Graph Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Natural Language Action Spaces
extracted_data.agent_description: An agent that incrementally constructs and updates a Knowledge Graph of the game state and uses the KG as an internal structured memory to compute state representations and guide policy learning using A2C.
extracted_data.memory_type: structured knowledge graph (dynamic KG)
extracted_data.memory_integration: dynamically updated KG representing the current state/world; KG used to compute state representation for policy/value estimation (integrated with A2C training)
extracted_data.key_findings: Mentioned as the first proposal to use a dynamically updated knowledge graph to represent the current game universe; cited as a prior approach that uses explicit structured memory to handle partial observability and large action spaces.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Described as a more complex architecture compared to the paper's proposed DBERT-based approach; the current paper emphasizes that similar or better performance can sometimes be achieved without an explicit KG by leveraging pre-trained language models, implying KG approaches add architectural complexity and may require additional supervision or components.

extracted_data.name_full: Deep Reinforcement Learning with Stacked Hierarchical Attention (SHA-KG)
extracted_data.brief_description: A KG-based agent that reasons over an explicit knowledge graph using hierarchical attention (low- and high-level) on graph subcomponents to produce a game-state representation, and decodes actions with a recurrent decoder.
extracted_data.citation_title: Deep Reinforcement Learning with Stacked Hierarchical Attention for Text-based Games
extracted_data.agent_description: An agent that maintains a knowledge graph and applies stacked hierarchical attention over subcomponents and the full graph to compute state representations; actions are selected by recurrent decoding (GRUs) conditioned on that representation.
extracted_data.memory_type: structured knowledge graph (dynamic KG)
extracted_data.memory_integration: KG is maintained and attended over with low- and high-level attention to compute a state representation; recurrent decoder (GRU) conditioned on KG-derived representation selects actions; trained with A2C and an auxiliary valid-action prediction task.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: See Table 1 in this paper (SHA-KG column) — example: Zork1 raw score 34.5 (as reported in the comparison table in this paper).
extracted_data.key_findings: SHA-KG is cited as a state-of-the-art KG-based method that reasons explicitly on a graph and attains competitive scores; the present paper notes that a pre-trained LM + DRRN can outperform or match such KG-based methods without explicit KG construction.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: KG-based systems are relatively sophisticated and require graph construction/maintenance and additional reasoning modules; the paper suggests pre-trained LMs provide strong linguistic/world priors that can reduce the need for explicit KG machinery.

extracted_data.name_full: Q*BERT (KG updated via Open-Domain QA + intrinsic motivation)
extracted_data.brief_description: An approach that augments a knowledge graph via open-domain question-answering to add facts to the KG, combined with an intrinsic-motivation reward to encourage structured exploration in text games.
extracted_data.citation_title: How to Avoid Being Eaten by a Grue: Structured Exploration Strategies for Textual Worlds
extracted_data.agent_description: An agent that updates its KG using an open-domain QA method to extract additional information, and uses intrinsic motivation rewards to encourage structured exploration; the augmented KG informs policy learning/decision-making.
extracted_data.memory_type: structured knowledge graph augmented by open-domain QA
extracted_data.memory_integration: KG is updated using an open-domain QA mechanism to add additional facts; an intrinsic motivation reward is added to encourage exploration and to create dependencies in the KG for better planning.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: See Table 1 in this paper (Q*BERT column) — example: Zork1 max score reported 41.6 (as reported in the comparison table in this paper).
extracted_data.key_findings: Mentioned as a KG-updating approach that supplements graph memory with QA-extracted facts and intrinsic rewards; used as a baseline in the paper's comparisons, and DBERT-DRRN attains comparable or better scores on several games without explicit KG updates.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Requires an additional QA component and intrinsic reward shaping; paper emphasizes that comparable performance can sometimes be achieved by leveraging pre-trained language models without explicit graph construction, implying Q*BERT's complexity may not always be necessary.

extracted_data.brief_description: An agent that addresses partial observability by retrieving relevant past observations (object-centric retrieval) and applying attention over retrieved context; candidate actions are generated by converting templates to QA-style queries and then scored by a DRRN-like component.
extracted_data.citation_title: Interactive Fiction Game Playing as Multi-Paragraph Reading Comprehension with Reinforcement Learning
extracted_data.agent_description: A DQN-based approach that treats the game as a multi-paragraph reading comprehension task: it retrieves and attends to relevant past observations to mitigate partial observability and uses QA-formatted templates to generate and score candidate actions.
extracted_data.memory_type: retrieval-augmented episodic memory (object-centric retrieval of past observations)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Retrieves relevant past observations (object-centric) to address partial observability; an attention mechanism focuses on the most relevant retrieved context which is then used by the policy/value estimator (DQN/DRRN-style) for action scoring.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: See Table 1 in this paper (MPRC-DQN column) — example scores reported here include Zork1 raw score 38.3, Library 17.7, Ludicorp 19.7, Spellbrkr 25 (as reported in the comparison table).
extracted_data.key_findings: Cited as a method that explicitly handles partial observability using retrieval of past observations and attention; the authors note that DBERT-DRRN (their pre-trained LM + DRRN) outperforms or is comparable to MPRC-DQN on average, suggesting strong language priors can partially substitute for explicit retrieval-memory mechanisms.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: While retrieval helps partial observability, the paper points out overall sample inefficiency of RL and that better language understanding (via pre-trained LMs) can achieve competitive results without explicit episodic retrieval; MPRC-DQN still serves as an effective baseline for retrieval-based memory augmentation.

extracted_data.brief_description: Autoregressive GPT2-medium language models fine-tuned to generate actions in the ALFWorld text-game environment; trained via supervised action modeling on expert demonstrations and augmented by iterated action modeling (replaying successful trajectories) to improve performance with few demonstrations.
extracted_data.agent_description: GPT2-medium (345M) autoregressive models used as agents: GPT is fine-tuned on the full demonstration set, GPT_partial is fine-tuned on a small subset (42 demos), and GPT*_partial is GPT_partial further trained with iterated action modeling (IAM) using successful interaction trajectories.
extracted_data.memory_type: context-window concatenation (short-term context) and dataset-based replay of successful trajectories (experience replay via iterated action modeling)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Short-term memory: goal + past observations and actions are concatenated (with modality markers) into the model input up to a max sequence length (1000 tokens) with a sliding context window; Longer-term memory / experience: successful interaction trajectories are stored and added to the imitation training data (iterated action modeling) to fine-tune the same action-generation head.
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: GPT*_partial (using iterated action modeling): ALFWorld goals success rate — 69% (seen split), 60% (unseen split). Human-annotated goals (out-of-distribution) success rate — 37% (unseen split).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: GPT_partial (before iterated action modeling): ALFWorld goals success rate — 47% (seen split), 40% (unseen split). Full-data GPT (no IAM, but trained on full demonstrations) achieves 91% (seen) and 95% (unseen).
extracted_data.key_findings: 1) Concatenating recent observations/actions into the transformer context plus fine-tuning on demonstrations yields strong action-generation agents. 2) Iterated Action Modeling (treating successful trajectories as extra demonstrations) improves a limited-demonstration agent (GPT_partial) by 22% (seen) and 20% (unseen), allowing GPT*_partial to recover a large fraction of the full-data GPT performance while using only ~1.2% of demonstrations. 3) This combination (context-window + IAM) obviates the need for an explicit external memory module in this work.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Memory is implemented only as concatenated context limited by the model's max sequence length (1000 tokens) and a sliding window, so long-horizon history beyond that is not explicitly retained. The longer-term "memory" is dataset-level (replaying successful trajectories) rather than an external or retrieval-augmented memory system. No experiments compare to explicit external memory architectures, and performance still lags behind the full-demonstration model when demonstrations are scarce.

extracted_data.brief_description: A GPT2-small based action-generation model fine-tuned on gameplay transcripts to produce candidate actions conditioned on recent observations and actions, used to prune the action space for downstream RL agents.
extracted_data.citation_title: Keep CALM and explore: Language models for action generation in text-based games.
extracted_data.agent_description: GPT2-small (117M) fine-tuned on a corpus of gameplay transcripts to generate small lists of action candidates from the last few observations/actions; candidate list is passed to game-specific RL models to pick actions.
extracted_data.memory_type: short-term context window (conditioning on the last few observations and actions)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Generation conditioned on a short recent context; CALM provides a candidate set of actions based on recent history which downstream components select among.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: Various text-based games (ClubFloyd dataset); attempted in ALFWorld (preliminary experiments mentioned)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Not reported numerically for ALFWorld here; authors of the present paper state preliminary experiments with CALM in ALFWorld showed it was unable to produce valid actions in terms of grammar and task completion (negative qualitative result).
extracted_data.key_findings: CALM demonstrates the idea of using a language model to prune action spaces by conditioning on recent context, but according to this paper, CALM did not transfer effectively to ALFWorld and failed to produce valid actions in that environment.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: CALM relies on short-term context and a transferable action-generation prior; it struggled with environment-specific generative grammars and task completion in ALFWorld, indicating limitations when general LM-based action models are applied without game-specific demonstrations or adaptation.

extracted_data.brief_description: A pretrained GPT-2 conditional language model trained on human gameplay transcripts to generate context-conditioned action candidate lists for text-adventure agents; candidates are re-ranked by a DRRN RL agent to maximize rewards.
extracted_data.agent_description: CALM is a language-model-based action generator (GPT-2 fine-tuned on ClubFloyd transcripts) that, given a short context, emits a ranked set of candidate text actions; these top-k candidates are supplied to a Deep Reinforcement Relevance Network (DRRN) which learns Q-values over candidates and selects actions during gameplay.
extracted_data.memory_type: short-term context window (local history)
extracted_data.memory_integration: context window concatenation: the LM conditions on a short context c (practically (o_{t-1}, a_{t-1}, o_t) or equivalently optionally longer histories) concatenated into the LM input to generate actions
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: avg.normalized score: 9.4% (average normalized score across 28 Jericho games when CALM (GPT-2) generates top-30 actions and DRRN is trained on them)
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: CALM (n-gram) baseline avg.normalized score: 5.5% (serves as a context-limited / simpler-generation comparison reported in the paper)
extracted_data.key_findings: Conditioning a powerful pretrained LM (GPT-2) on a short context window yields more diverse and complex action candidates (higher recall of gold/admissible actions and gold-action recall), which when combined with RL (DRRN) produces substantially better gameplay than a simple n-gram action generator; CALM (GPT-2) attained 9.4% avg.normalized score, outperforming the n-gram CALM (5.5%) and other handicapped-free baselines, and in some games even outperformed agents with access to ground-truth admissible actions.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Memory use is limited to a short context window (practically 2-step history); there is no explicit long-term or episodic memory module. CALM's action set size k trades off precision vs recall (larger k finds more admissible actions but lowers precision). RL (DRRN) sometimes fails to fully exploit high-scoring trajectories seen during exploration, suggesting the reduced action space alone does not guarantee optimal learning. CALM is not fine-tuned during RL to avoid overfitting, which may limit adaptation to specific games.
