extracted_data.brief_description: A bi-gram language-model-based action generator trained on human gameplay transcripts that produces verb-object style action candidates, used as the candidate set for a DRRN agent.
extracted_data.agent_description: An action generator based on n-gram counts (best performing was bigram with smoothing) that enumerates verb phrases and detected nouns to form candidate actions; the candidate list is passed to DRRN which learns Q-values and selects actions.
extracted_data.memory_type: current-observation conditioning only (no history)
extracted_data.memory_integration: stateless generation: the n-gram model is trained/used conditioned only on the current observation (o_t) and a restricted verb/object product set, not on prior steps
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: avg.normalized score: 5.5% (average normalized score across 28 Jericho games when CALM (n-gram) generates top-30 actions and DRRN is trained on them)
extracted_data.key_findings: The n-gram CALM captures common simple actions (primarily single- or two-token verb-object patterns) and works reasonably for easy games, but it cannot generate many complex multi-token actions (e.g. 'open case with key'), leading to lower recall of gold actions and worse gameplay vs the GPT-2 CALM.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Limited expressivity due to the verb+object generation assumption; lower recall of complex admissible/gold actions; conditions only on current observation (no short-term history), which reduces contextual action generation capability.

extracted_data.brief_description: A baseline RL agent that constructs and uses a game knowledge graph to augment state representation and constrain the action space during learning.
extracted_data.citation_title: Graph constrained reinforcement learning for natural language action spaces
extracted_data.agent_description: An actor-critic RL agent that builds an evolving knowledge graph representing discovered game entities and relations; the graph augments the agent's state representation and constrains/filters generated actions during learning.
extracted_data.memory_type: structured knowledge graph (state/episodic memory-like representation)
extracted_data.memory_integration: the game knowledge graph augments the state representation used by the policy and is used to constrain the types of actions produced/considered during learning (graph-augmented state encoding and action filtering)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: avg.normalized score: 10.8% (reported baseline performance from the paper's comparisons)
extracted_data.key_findings: Using an explicit structured memory (knowledge graph) improves the agent's ability to constrain and explore the action space; KG-A2C is a competitive baseline that, in the Jericho benchmark, attains a higher average normalized score than some handicap-free baselines but in this paper is still outperformed by DRRN on average and is roughly comparable to CALM (GPT-2) in some games.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: KG-A2C as reported relies on the admissible-action handicap during learning in prior work (used as auxiliary guidance) and building / maintaining the knowledge graph is an additional overhead; direct comparisons with/without the graph are not provided in this paper.

extracted_data.brief_description: An LLM-driven agent that performs episodic reflection: after interactions it analyzes its own chain-of-thought traces and produces reflective summaries that are stored for later retrieval to improve future planning and decision-making in text-adventure style environments.
extracted_data.agent_description: Central LLM agent that interleaves execution with reflective analysis of past trajectories; uses short-term in-episode context for immediate reasoning and writes reflective summaries of episodes to a long-term memory for later retrieval and re-planning.
extracted_data.memory_type: episodic working memory + reflective long-term memory (external summaries)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Reflection summaries are written to an external memory store and retrieved into the LLM prompt for subsequent episodes; in-episode observations are kept in-context for immediate decisions.
extracted_data.key_findings: Reflection (writing and later retrieving summaries of past episodes) enables agents to iteratively improve planning and recover from past mistakes in interactive/text-adventure environments; reflection introduces a temporal learning loop that augments LLM reasoning across episodes.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Survey notes limited discussion of quantitative comparisons; open questions include when to consolidate experiences, how to structure retrieved reflections to avoid misleading or stale guidance, and retrieval relevance selection.

extracted_data.brief_description: A prompting-style agent design that interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with environment actions, enabling LLMs to plan and act step-by-step in text-based interactive tasks.
extracted_data.agent_description: LLM agent using instruction-guided chain-of-thought that alternates between reasoning traces and concrete actions; relies primarily on in-context working memory (the current prompt + recent observations) rather than an external long-term memory store.
extracted_data.memory_type: working memory (in-context / short-term)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Context window concatenation: recent observations and intermediate thoughts are kept in the prompt (working memory) to guide subsequent steps; no persistent external memory described in the survey summary.
extracted_data.key_findings: Interleaving CoT with actions helps planning in text-adventure tasks, but chain-of-thought increases stochasticity and can cause temporal inconsistency unless complemented by maintenance strategies; ReAct itself generally uses only in-episode context rather than persistent memory.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: CoT can amplify stochasticity and lead to inconsistent step-to-step behavior; lack of long-term consolidation limits progress on long-horizon or multi-episode tasks.

extracted_data.brief_description: An agent that uses in-context dialogue as working memory and leverages exploration experience to fine-tune model parameters, effectively combining short-term in-prompt memory with parametric (fine-tuned) long-term memory.
extracted_data.agent_description: Agent applied to VirtualHome that maintains immediate dialogue/state in the prompt (working memory) and consolidates exploration experiences into parametric memory by fine-tuning the model on those experiences (long-term memory).
extracted_data.memory_type: working memory (in-context) and parametric long-term memory via fine-tuning
extracted_data.memory_integration: Short-term state and dialogue are maintained by prompt/context concatenation; long-term knowledge is integrated by fine-tuning the model on collected exploration trajectories (parametric storage).
extracted_data.key_findings: Embedding exploration experience via fine-tuning can capture reusable behaviors and world knowledge beyond what fits in a single prompt, while in-context dialogue supports immediate conversational coherence and short-horizon decisions.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Fine-tuning introduces costs (data, compute) and risks (catastrophic forgetting, reduced adaptability); survey does not report detailed numeric comparisons within this work.

extracted_data.brief_description: A planner-style LLM agent for embodied instruction-following tasks that uses in-episode context and retrieves exemplar plans from a long-term store to assist planning and re-planning.
extracted_data.agent_description: An LLM-based planner for tasks like ALFRED that keeps recent observations in-context and augments planning by retrieving exemplar (previously successful) plans from an external memory to seed or guide current plan generation.
extracted_data.memory_type: exemplar plan retrieval (external long-term memory)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Retrieval-augmented prompting: exemplar plans are retrieved from external storage (memory bank) and concatenated into the prompt to guide current planning and re-planning.
extracted_data.key_findings: Retrieving exemplar plans helps planning and re-planning in instruction-conditioned embodied/textual tasks by providing concrete examples to the LLM, improving its ability to decompose and sequence actions.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Effective exemplar selection and prompt-budget trade-offs (context length) limit how many examples can be used; survey does not provide exact performance deltas.

extracted_data.brief_description: A multi-agent simulation architecture where each agent maintains a streaming, hierarchical (tree) long-term memory with metadata and importance scoring; memories are consolidated via LLM-guided reflection and retrieved by relevance to drive long-horizon, socially coherent behavior.
extracted_data.agent_description: Agents pair a central LLM with an external hierarchical memory tree and metadata-driven consolidation: incoming observations are importance-scored, summarized by an LLM when thresholds are exceeded, and appended into a streaming long-term memory that is later retrieved by similarity and metadata weighting.
extracted_data.memory_type: streaming hierarchical long-term memory (tree of chunks) with metadata; working memory via context
extracted_data.memory_integration: Signal-triggered consolidation (importance scoring) writes summaries into a memory tree; retrieval uses recency/importance-weighted ranking to select memories which are inserted into prompts for ongoing reasoning.
extracted_data.key_findings: Hierarchical streaming memories with importance-based consolidation allow agents to maintain long-term continuity and produce coherent long-horizon narratives and social behaviors; metadata-weighted retrieval approximates human-like forgetting curves to prioritize salient memories.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Scalability of memory growth and retrieval, design of importance scoring thresholds, and ensuring retrieval precision (avoiding irrelevant/stale memories) remain open problems.

extracted_data.brief_description: LLM agents applied to the text-based social deduction game Werewolf that use in-episode experience for immediate decisions and reflective experience for retrieval to support role fidelity and coordination across episodes.
extracted_data.agent_description: LLM-based agents for Werewolf that maintain immediate in-episode observations/dialogue in context and also store reflective experiences from past games which are retrieved to inform beliefs, role-consistent behavior, and coordination.
extracted_data.memory_type: working memory (in-episode) + reflective long-term memory (external retrieval)
extracted_data.memory_integration: Reflective experiences are summarized and stored externally; retrieval is used to provide prior episode-derived beliefs and role-history into prompts during new games.
extracted_data.key_findings: Using reflective retrieval improves role fidelity and helps agents maintain consistent personas and strategies across rounds in a text-based multi-agent social deduction setting.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Reconciling conflicting messages from other agents, partial observability, and ensuring retrieval relevance and timeliness in fast multi-round games are highlighted as challenges.

extracted_data.name_full: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents
extracted_data.brief_description: An autonomous framework that generates structured attribute-value augmentations for LLM agent memories and uses attribute-based filtering and embedding-based retrieval (with priority or basic aggregation) to improve retrieval and downstream performance on QA, conversational recommendation, and event summarization.
extracted_data.agent_description: An LLM agent augmented with an autonomous memory augmentation pipeline (attribute mining, annotation/prioritization, and retrieval). Memory entries are annotated with attribute-value pairs (entity- or conversation-centric; turn- or session-level). Retrieval is via (a) attribute-based filtering using matching attributes or (b) embedding-based similarity over attribute embeddings (Titan Text Embeddings indexed with FAISS). Retrieved memory entries are integrated into the current prompt/context (top-k). Supports Basic and Priority augmentation ordering.
extracted_data.model_name: Backbone LLMs used for augmentation and tasks include Claude-3-Sonnet (primary backbone for augmentation), Mistral v1, Llama v3, and Claude-3-Haiku; Titan Text Embedding used for embedding-based retrieval; FAISS for index/search.
extracted_data.memory_type: Structured attribute-augmented memory (attribute-value augmentations attached to episodic/dialogue memory entries) with optional dense vector encodings for embedding retrieval.
extracted_data.memory_integration: Memory instances are annotated with generated attributes and stored; retrieval is performed by attribute-based filtering (match attribute sets) or embedding-based similarity over attribute embeddings (averaging attribute embeddings or single embedding of all augmentations) and the retrieved top-k turns/entries are concatenated into the model context for generation.
extracted_data.benchmark_name: LoCoMo (Question Answering & Event Summarization), LLM-REDIAL (Conversational Recommendation)
extracted_data.performance_with_memory: Question Answering F1 (attribute-based/embedding settings): example: MemInsight (Claude-3-SonnetPriority) overall F1 = 30.1% vs baseline 26.1% (Claude-3-Sonnet no augmentation). Embedding-based retrieval Recall@k=5 on LoCoMo: MemInsight (Claude-3-SonnetPriority) overall recall = 60.5% vs DPR RAG baseline = 26.5% (Table 2). Conversational recommendation: genre-match R@1 improved from baseline 0.320 to 0.400 (MemInsight Claude-3-Sonnet embedding); LLM-based persuasiveness increases up to ~12-14% in embedding-based settings (paper reports up to 14% persuasiveness boost in abstract / up to 12% increase in highly persuasive recommendations in embedding-based retrieval).
extracted_data.performance_without_memory: Baseline results reported using same backbone but without MemInsight augmentation: QA baseline (Claude-3-Sonnet) overall F1 = 26.1%; DPR RAG baseline recall@5 = 26.5% (Table 1 & 2). Conversational recommendation baseline genre-match R@1 = 0.320 (Table 4).
extracted_data.key_findings: Autonomous structured augmentations (attribute-value pairs) improve retrieval relevance and downstream performance: priority-based augmentation outperforms basic aggregation, embedding-based retrieval over attribute embeddings yields large recall gains over DPR RAG baselines (paper reports ~34% recall improvement in abstract / large absolute gains in Table 2), and augmentation reduces the amount of memory retrieved while preserving or improving recommendation persuasiveness and QA accuracy. Turn-level augmentations can yield more precise event summaries than session-level.
extracted_data.challenges_or_limitations: Augmentation quality depends on the backbone LLM used for attribute generation (less capable models produce less consistent or more generic attributes); occasional abstract/generic annotations (~0.86% of annotations were non-specific per DeepEval hallucination analysis); some categories (temporal and adversarial questions) see DPR or other baselines performing slightly better; current implementation limited to text-only inputs.
